# FA Cup Finals Won by Tottenham Hotspur F.C.

**1901 FA Cup Final**
Tottenham Hotspur won their first FA Cup in 1901, defeating Sheffield United 3-1 in a replay after the initial match ended 2-2. This victory made them the only non-League club to win the FA Cup since the formation of the Football League in 1888.

**1921 FA Cup Final**
Tottenham defeated Wolverhampton Wanderers 1-0 at Stamford Bridge. This was their second FA Cup triumph and came during their time in the Second Division, making them the first club from outside the top division to win the FA Cup since the Football League's expansion.

**1961 FA Cup Final**
Spurs beat Leicester City 2-0 at Wembley Stadium. This victory completed the famous League and FA Cup Double in the 1960-61 season, making Tottenham the first club in the 20th century to achieve this feat.

**1962 FA Cup Final**
Tottenham successfully defended their FA Cup title by defeating Burnley 3-1 at Wembley. This made them the first club since Newcastle United in 1952 to retain the trophy.

**1967 FA Cup Final**
Spurs defeated Chelsea 2-1 at Wembley in the first all-London FA Cup final. This match is considered the beginning of the intense rivalry between Chelsea and Tottenham, and featured goals from Jimmy Robertson and Frank Saul for Tottenham.

**1981 FA Cup Final**
Tottenham beat Manchester City 3-2 in a replay after the initial match ended 1-1. Ricky Villa scored one of the most famous goals in FA Cup final history with a memorable solo run in the replay at Wembley.

**1982 FA Cup Final**
Spurs defeated Queens Park Rangers 1-0 in a replay after a 1-1 draw. Glenn Hoddle scored the winning penalty in the replay, giving Tottenham back-to-back FA Cup victories.

**1991 FA Cup Final**
Tottenham beat Nottingham Forest 2-1 in the final at Wembley. Paul Gascoigne's performance in the semi-final against Arsenal is remembered, though he was injured early in the final itself, with goals from Paul Stewart and Des Walker (own goal) securing the victory.

# Buildings Designed by William Thomas

**St. Michael's Cathedral Basilica, Toronto** - Completed in 1848, this Gothic Revival Roman Catholic cathedral is one of William Thomas's most significant ecclesiastical works in Toronto. The cathedral features a tall spire and remains an important landmark in downtown Toronto.

**Brock's Monument, Queenston Heights** - Thomas designed the second and current monument (completed 1856) commemorating Major General Sir Isaac Brock. This 56-meter tall column stands on the Niagara Escarpment and is a prominent memorial to the War of 1812 hero.

**St. Lawrence Hall, Toronto** - Completed in 1850, this Renaissance Revival building served as Toronto's premier public meeting place and concert hall. It features an impressive third-floor Great Hall and remains a significant heritage building in the St. Lawrence Market area.

**St. Paul's Anglican Church, Toronto** - Built in 1841-1842, this Gothic Revival church on Bloor Street East was one of Thomas's early Toronto commissions. The church showcases his skill in ecclesiastical Gothic architecture.

**The Seventh Post Office, Toronto** - Designed in 1851-1853, this building served as Toronto's main post office and featured Thomas's characteristic Italianate styling. It was located on Toronto Street and represented his work in civic architecture.

**Don Jail, Toronto** - Completed in 1864, this imposing Romanesque Revival prison was one of Thomas's major institutional works. The radial-plan jail served Toronto for over a century and is now a heritage building.

**Oakham House, Toronto** - Built around 1848, this brick residence is one of the oldest surviving buildings in downtown Toronto. It demonstrates Thomas's residential design work and is now part of Ryerson University campus.

**St. George the Martyr Church, Toronto** - This Gothic Revival Anglican church was designed by Thomas and built in 1844-1845. Located in what is now the Grange Park area, it served the growing Anglican community.

**Commercial Bank of the Midland District, Kingston** - Thomas designed this bank building in Kingston, Ontario, showcasing his work in commercial architecture. The building reflected the prosperity of mid-19th century Upper Canada.

**Residence for John Spread Baldwin, Toronto** - Thomas designed this substantial private residence for a prominent Toronto citizen, demonstrating his work in domestic architecture for the city's elite.

**Yorkville Town Hall, Toronto** - Before Yorkville was annexed by Toronto, Thomas designed its town hall building, serving the independent village's civic needs in the mid-19th century.

**Various commercial buildings on King Street and Yonge Street, Toronto** - Thomas designed numerous commercial structures along Toronto's main business thoroughfares during the 1840s-1850s, contributing significantly to the city's Victorian streetscape.

# Competitions Won by Mercedes

Based on the question about competitions won by Mercedes (referring to Mercedes-Benz in motorsport, particularly Formula One), here are the different parts/components of competitions they have won:

**Formula One World Constructors' Championships**
Mercedes has won eight consecutive World Constructors' Championship titles from 2014 to 2021 under team principal Toto Wolff. This represents the team championship in Formula One, awarded to the constructor with the most points accumulated by their drivers throughout the season.

**Formula One World Drivers' Championships**
Mercedes drivers have won multiple World Drivers' Championship titles during the team's dominant era. Lewis Hamilton won six of his seven titles while driving for Mercedes (2014, 2015, 2017, 2018, 2019, 2020), and Nico Rosberg won one title in 2016.

**Formula E World Teams' Championships**
Mercedes-EQ won two Formula E World Teams' Championship titles in the all-electric racing series. This demonstrates Mercedes' success beyond traditional Formula One racing in emerging electric motorsport categories.

**Individual Grand Prix Race Wins**
Mercedes has accumulated numerous individual race victories in Formula One. Lewis Hamilton alone has won 105 Formula One races, with the majority coming during his time with Mercedes since 2013.

**Pole Positions**
Mercedes has secured countless pole positions in Formula One qualifying sessions. Lewis Hamilton holds the record with 104 pole positions, most achieved with Mercedes, demonstrating the team's qualifying pace superiority.

**Podium Finishes**
Mercedes drivers have achieved hundreds of podium finishes (top-three results) in Formula One races. Hamilton alone has 203 podium finishes, with the majority coming during the Mercedes era of dominance.

**Fastest Laps**
Mercedes has won numerous fastest lap awards during races, which are awarded to the driver who completes the quickest single lap during a Grand Prix. This demonstrates both car performance and driver skill.

**24 Hours Nürburgring Category Wins**
Toto Wolff, now Mercedes team principal, won his category in the 1994 24 Hours Nürburgring. Mercedes has also had success in this endurance racing event through various programs.

**World Sportscar Championship Races**
Mercedes (as Sauber Mercedes) won races in the World Sportscar Championship, including victories with Michael Schumacher as a driver in 1990 before his Formula One career.

**Triple Crown Components (Monaco Grand Prix)**
McLaren-Mercedes partnership contributed to wins at the Monaco Grand Prix, one component of motorsport's Triple Crown. Mercedes engines powered McLaren to numerous Monaco victories during their partnership.

**Sprint Race Wins**
Mercedes has won Formula One sprint races, the shorter Saturday races introduced in recent seasons that determine grid positions and award points. These are distinct competition formats within the modern F1 calendar.

**Constructor Points Records**
Mercedes has set records for total constructor points in single seasons during their period of dominance. These point totals represent cumulative success across all races in a championship season.

# Members of the Continental Navy

**Esek Hopkins**
Esek Hopkins was appointed as the first Commander-in-Chief of the Continental Navy in 1775. He commanded the fleet's first major operation in 1776, leading an expedition to the Bahamas, and his flagship was USS Alfred.

**John Paul Jones**
Often called the "Father of the American Navy," John Paul Jones was one of the most famous Continental Navy officers. He commanded several ships including USS Ranger and USS Bonhomme Richard, and won the famous Battle of Flamborough Head in 1779.

**Silas Talbot**
Silas Talbot served as an officer in both the Continental Army and Continental Navy during the Revolutionary War. He later commanded USS Constitution from 1799 to 1801 and was a member of the Society of the Cincinnati.

**Nicholas Biddle**
Nicholas Biddle was a Continental Navy captain who commanded USS Randolph. He was killed in action in 1778 when his ship exploded during battle with HMS Yarmouth off Barbados.

**John Barry**
John Barry, known as the "Father of the American Navy" alongside Jones, commanded several Continental Navy ships including USS Lexington, USS Raleigh, and USS Alliance. He won the final naval battle of the Revolutionary War in 1783.

**Lambert Wickes**
Lambert Wickes commanded USS Reprisal and conducted successful raids against British shipping in European waters. He worked closely with Benjamin Franklin while operating from French ports and was lost at sea in 1777.

**Gustavus Conyngham**
Gustavus Conyngham was a highly successful Continental Navy captain who commanded USS Revenge. He captured dozens of British merchant vessels and was known as the "Dunkirk Pirate" by the British.

**Joshua Barney**
Joshua Barney served in the Continental Navy from age 15, eventually commanding several vessels. He was captured multiple times by the British but escaped and continued fighting throughout the war.

**Samuel Nicholas**
Samuel Nicholas was the first commissioned officer of the Continental Marines, the naval infantry force of the Continental Navy. He recruited the first Marines in Philadelphia in 1775 and led them in several amphibious operations.

**Abraham Whipple**
Abraham Whipple was a Continental Navy commodore who commanded USS Providence and USS Columbus. He led successful raids against British shipping and participated in Hopkins' Bahamas expedition.

**John Manley**
John Manley commanded several Continental Navy vessels including USS Hancock. He was one of the most successful American naval commanders, capturing numerous British prizes during the war.

**Dudley Saltonstall**
Dudley Saltonstall commanded USS Trumbull and later led the disastrous Penobscot Expedition in 1779. He was court-martialed and dismissed from service after that failed operation.

**Hoysted Hacker**
Hoysted Hacker served as a Continental Navy officer and commanded USS Providence under John Paul Jones. He later commanded several other vessels during the war.

**Thomas Truxtun**
Thomas Truxtun served in the Continental Navy as a privateer captain and later became a prominent officer in the United States Navy. He commanded USS Constellation during the Quasi-War with France.

**James Nicholson**
James Nicholson was the senior captain of the Continental Navy and commanded USS Trumbull and USS Virginia. He was the highest-ranking naval officer by seniority during much of the war.

**Seth Harding**
Seth Harding commanded USS Defence and later USS Confederacy. He successfully captured several British vessels and fought numerous engagements during the Revolutionary War.

**John Paul**
John Paul (before adding "Jones" to his name) served on Continental Navy vessels in the early stages of the war before his famous exploits under his full adopted name.

**Thomas Thompson**
Thomas Thompson commanded USS Raleigh and participated in several naval operations. He was later court-martialed for losing his ship but was acquitted.

**Hector McNeill**
Hector McNeill commanded USS Boston and participated in various naval operations. He served alongside John Manley in several engagements against British forces.

**Samuel Tucker**
Samuel Tucker commanded USS Boston and USS Hancock during the war. He successfully transported John Adams to France in 1778 and captured numerous British prizes.

Based on the passages and my encyclopedic knowledge, here are the films that Walter Hill both directed and wrote (or co-wrote):

**Streets of Fire (1984)**
This rock & roll fable was directed by Walter Hill from a screenplay he co-wrote with Larry Gross. The film stars Michael Paré and Diane Lane in a story combining 1950s culture with 1980s aesthetics.

**48 Hrs. (1982)**
Hill directed this buddy cop action comedy and co-wrote the screenplay with Larry Gross, Steven E. de Souza, and Roger Spottiswoode. The film starred Nick Nolte and Eddie Murphy in his film debut.

**The Warriors (1979)**
Hill directed and co-wrote the screenplay for this cult classic about a New York City gang trying to make it home after being framed for murder. The film was based on Sol Yurick's novel and adapted by Hill and David Shaber.

**The Driver (1978)**
Hill both wrote and directed this neo-noir crime thriller starring Ryan O'Neal as a getaway driver. The minimalist screenplay was entirely Hill's work.

**Hard Times (1975)**
Hill's directorial debut, which he co-wrote with Bryan Gindoff and Bruce Henstell. The film stars Charles Bronson as a bare-knuckle fighter during the Great Depression.

**Southern Comfort (1981)**
Hill directed and co-wrote this thriller with Michael Kane and David Giler. The film follows National Guard soldiers on a training exercise in the Louisiana bayou.

**Extreme Prejudice (1987)**
Hill directed and contributed to the screenplay of this action film starring Nick Nolte. He worked on the script with Deric Washburn and Harry Kleiner.

**Last Man Standing (1996)**
Hill wrote and directed this Prohibition-era action film starring Bruce Willis, which was a remake of Akira Kurosawa's Yojimbo.

**"Enjoy Enjaami"** - This independent single released in 2021 featured Dhee and Arivu, celebrating agricultural heritage and becoming a cultural phenomenon. The song garnered hundreds of millions of views and brought attention to social issues through music.

**"Kaavaalaa"** - Created for the 2023 film *Jailer* starring Rajinikanth, this peppy number featured Tamannaah Bhatia and became a massive hit. The song was sung by Shilpa Rao and Anirudh Ravichander (though Vishal contributed to the soundtrack).

**"Nee Paartha Vizhigal"** - A melodious romantic track from the 2014 Tamil film *Vil Ambu*, this song showcased Vishal's versatility in composing soulful melodies. The film starred Nagarjuna and Sri Divya.

**"Kannamma"** - Composed for the 2019 Tamil film *Kaappaan* starring Suriya, this romantic number was well-received by audiences. The song featured vocals by Sid Sriram and became popular on music streaming platforms.

**"Aathichudi"** - Another track from *Enai Noki Paayum Thota* (2019), this song demonstrated Vishal's ability to blend traditional Tamil literary elements with contemporary music. The film starred Dhanush and Megha Akash.

**"Maruvaarthai"** - A soulful melody from the 2019 film *Enai Noki Paayum Thota*, this romantic track was sung by Sid Sriram. The song became popular for its emotional depth and musical arrangement.

**"Thangamey"** - Composed for the 2017 Tamil film *Naanum Rowdy Dhaan* (though primarily scored by Anirudh, Vishal has worked on various projects), this showcases his collaborative work in the industry.

**"Aagasam"** - A track from *Sarvam Thaala Mayam* (2019), where Vishal worked alongside A.R. Rahman, contributing to the film's musical landscape. The film explored the world of Carnatic music and mridangam.

**"Simtaangaran"** - Composed for the 2018 film *Sarkar* starring Vijay, this political anthem became hugely popular. The song was sung by Bamba Bakya, Srinidhi Venkatesh, and others, featuring powerful lyrics.

**"Oru Viral Puratchi"** - Another impactful track from *Sarkar* (2018), this song addressed social and political themes. The composition reflected Vishal's ability to create music that resonates with contemporary issues.

**"CEO in the House"** - A corporate-themed track from *Sarkar* (2018), this song featured modern electronic elements. The composition showcased Vishal's versatility in handling different musical genres.

**"Aalaporaan Thamizhan"** - Composed for the 2017 film *Mersal* starring Vijay, this patriotic Tamil anthem celebrated Tamil culture and heritage. The song was sung by Kailash Kher, Deepak, Pooja AV, and Sathyaprakash.

**"Neethane"** - A romantic melody from *Mersal* (2017), this song featured vocals by Shreya Ghoshal. The track became popular for its soothing composition and emotional appeal.

# Directors of Films Written by Robert Thom

**George Cukor** directed *Wild in the Country* (1961), a drama starring Elvis Presley. Robert Thom wrote the screenplay for this film about a troubled young man with literary talent who becomes involved with three different women.

**Roger Corman** directed *The Wild Angels* (1966), a biker exploitation film starring Peter Fonda and Nancy Sinatra. Thom wrote the screenplay for this influential counterculture film that helped establish the outlaw biker movie genre.

**Richard Rush** directed *The Savage Seven* (1968), another biker film written by Robert Thom. This film dealt with conflicts between a motorcycle gang and a Native American community.

**Otto Preminger** directed *Crazy Quilt* (1966), though this film had limited release. Thom contributed to the screenplay for this experimental drama.

**Stuart Rosenberg** directed *Move* (1970), a comedy-drama starring Elliott Gould. Robert Thom wrote the screenplay for this film about a playwright dealing with personal and professional upheaval.

**Jack Smight** directed *The Illustrated Man* (1969), based on Ray Bradbury's short story collection. Thom adapted the screenplay, weaving together several of Bradbury's science fiction tales starring Rod Steiger.

**Richard Sarafian** directed *Fragment of Fear* (1970), a psychological thriller. Thom wrote the screenplay for this British film about a reformed drug addict investigating his aunt's murder.

**Paul Wendkos** directed *Angel, Angel, Down We Go* (also known as *Cult of the Damned*, 1969). Thom both wrote the screenplay and co-produced this counterculture drama about a wealthy family corrupted by a rock singer and his entourage.

# Fictional Characters Created by Nick Cardy

**Black Manta**
Black Manta (David Milton Hyde) is a supervillain and the archenemy of Aquaman, first appearing in Aquaman #35 (September 1967). Created by writer Bob Haney and artist Nick Cardy, he is depicted as a ruthless underwater mercenary obsessed with destroying Aquaman and Atlantis, recognizable by his distinctive black armored suit with a large bug-eyed helmet.

**Mera**
Mera, also known as Aquawoman, is a superhero who first appeared in Aquaman #11 (September 1963). Created by Jack Miller and Nick Cardy, she is a royal native of the extradimensional realm of Xebel with natural hydrokinetic abilities and serves as Aquaman's chief love interest and queen consort of Atlantis.

**Aquagirl (Tula)**
Tula is the third and definitive version of Aquagirl, created by Bob Haney and Nick Cardy and debuting in Aquaman vol. 1 #33 (May–June 1967). An orphan adopted into one of the royal families of Atlantis, she became a romantic interest of the first Aqualad and served as a member of the Teen Titans until her death during Crisis on Infinite Earths.

**Teen Titans**
Nick Cardy was the artist who helped establish the visual identity of the Teen Titans team when they transitioned from their earlier appearances. His artwork defined the look of the team during their early adventures in the late 1960s and early 1970s.

**Lilith Clay**
Lilith is a precognitive superhero who was introduced in the Teen Titans series. Created during Nick Cardy's tenure as the primary artist for Teen Titans, she possessed psychic abilities and became a recurring member of the team.

**Hawk and Dove**
The original Hawk and Dove (Hank and Don Hall) were created by Steve Ditko and Steve Skeates, but Nick Cardy provided significant early artwork for the characters. These brothers represented opposing philosophies of aggression and pacifism while fighting crime together.

**Aqualad (Garth)**
While Aqualad was created by Robert Bernstein and Ramona Fradon in 1960, Nick Cardy significantly developed the character's visual appearance and storylines during his work on Aquaman. Garth served as Aquaman's teenage sidekick and later became known as Tempest.

**Ocean Master**
Ocean Master (Orm Marius), Aquaman's half-brother and frequent antagonist, received significant character development and visual refinement through Nick Cardy's artwork. The character is a recurring villain who seeks to rule Atlantis and defeat Aquaman.

# Scrubs Characters Who Debuted in "My First Day"

**John "J.D." Dorian**
The protagonist and narrator of Scrubs, played by Zach Braff. J.D. is a medical intern at Sacred Heart Hospital who begins his journey in the pilot episode, characterized by his frequent daydreams and internal monologues.

**Elliot Reid**
Played by Sarah Chalke, Elliot is a fellow medical intern who starts at Sacred Heart alongside J.D. She is characterized by her neurotic personality, competitive nature, and wealthy upbringing.

**Christopher Turk**
Portrayed by Donald Faison, Turk is J.D.'s best friend from college and a surgical intern. Their close friendship is a central element of the series from the very first episode.

**Perry Cox**
Played by John C. McGinley, Dr. Cox is an attending physician and J.D.'s reluctant mentor. He is known for his caustic wit, cynicism, and tough-love approach to teaching interns.

**Bob Kelso**
Portrayed by Ken Jenkins, Dr. Kelso is the Chief of Medicine at Sacred Heart Hospital. He is introduced as a seemingly friendly administrator who prioritizes the hospital's budget over patient care.

**Carla Espinosa**
Played by Judy Reyes, Carla is the head nurse at Sacred Heart. She serves as a maternal figure and voice of reason to the interns from the pilot episode.

**The Janitor**
Portrayed by Neil Flynn, the Janitor is introduced in the pilot episode when J.D. accidentally breaks a door. He becomes J.D.'s antagonist throughout the series, though his role was initially intended to be minor.

**Todd Quinlan**
Known as "The Todd," this surgical resident is characterized by his obsession with high-fives and inappropriate behavior. He appears in the pilot as a recurring character before becoming more prominent.

**Laverne Roberts**

wikipedia_claude-sonnet-4-5_self_contain:
# Architectural Structures Designed by Raymond Hood

**Tribune Tower**
The Tribune Tower in Chicago was designed by Raymond Hood and John Mead Howells, winning an international competition in 1922. This Neo-Gothic skyscraper, completed in 1925, became one of Hood's most celebrated early works and helped establish his reputation.

**American Radiator Building**
Located at 40 West 40th Street in New York City, this 23-story Art Deco skyscraper was designed by Hood and André Fouilhoux and completed in 1924. The building is notable for its distinctive black brick facade with gold-colored decorative elements on setbacks and pinnacles.

**Daily News Building**
Designed by Raymond Hood and John Mead Howells and completed in 1930, this 36-story Art Deco skyscraper at 220 East 42nd Street in Manhattan features a distinctive white-brick facade. The building is a National Historic Landmark and is famous for its rotunda lobby with a rotating painted globe.

**Rockefeller Center**
Hood was one of the principal architects of Rockefeller Center in New York City, working as part of the Associated Architects team in the 1930s. He played a crucial role in designing this iconic complex of Art Deco commercial buildings, which became one of the most significant architectural achievements of the era.

**McGraw-Hill Building**
Completed in 1931 at 330 West 42nd Street in Manhattan, this 33-story Art Deco skyscraper was designed by Hood, Godley & Fouilhoux. The building featured a distinctive blue-green terra cotta facade and horizontal banding that emphasized its modernist aesthetic.

**Masonic Temple and Scottish Rite Cathedral (Scranton, Pennsylvania)**
Designed by Hood early in his career around 1929, this building showcases his versatility in working with different architectural styles. The structure demonstrates Hood's ability to adapt his design approach to different building types and client needs.

**Beaux-Arts Apartments**
Located at 307-319 East 44th Street in New York City, this residential building was designed by Hood and completed in 1930. The apartment building demonstrates Hood's work in residential architecture alongside his more famous commercial projects.

**Ideal House**
Designed for the 1933 Century of Progress Exposition in Chicago, this experimental house showcased Hood's ideas about modern living and residential design. The structure represented his vision for future housing incorporating modern materials and design principles.

**National Radiator Company Building (London)**
Completed in 1928, this building in London's Mayfair district was designed by Hood for the British subsidiary of the American Radiator Company. The black and gold Art Deco structure brought Hood's distinctive architectural style to an international audience.

**Mori Memorial**
This memorial structure demonstrated Hood's ability to work on smaller-scale commemorative architecture. The design reflected his attention to detail and symbolic elements in architectural composition.

**Patterson House**
An early residential commission that showed Hood's development as an architect before his major commercial successes. This project helped establish his practice and design sensibilities.

# Athletic Contests Won by Lisa Raymond and Samantha Stosur

**2005 US Open Women's Doubles**
Lisa Raymond and Samantha Stosur won the 2005 US Open women's doubles championship, defeating Elena Dementieva and Flavia Pennetta in the final. This was their first Grand Slam title together as a partnership.

**2006 French Open Women's Doubles**
The pair captured the 2006 French Open women's doubles title at Roland Garros. This victory marked their second consecutive Grand Slam championship together.

**2006 US Open Women's Doubles**
Raymond and Stosur successfully defended their US Open title in 2006, winning their third Grand Slam doubles championship as a team. This demonstrated their dominance on hard courts during this period.

**2006 WTA Tour Championships Doubles**
The duo won the season-ending WTA Tour Championships (now known as the WTA Finals) in doubles competition in 2006. This prestigious title capped off their highly successful year together.

**2007 Italian Open (Rome) Doubles**
Raymond and Stosur won the Tier I Italian Open doubles title in Rome in 2007. This was one of several Premier-level tournaments they captured during their partnership.

**2007 Zurich Open Doubles**
The pair won the Zurich Open doubles championship in 2007, adding another Tier I title to their collection. This tournament was one of the most prestigious events on the women's tour.

**Multiple WTA Tour-level Doubles Titles (2005-2008)**
Throughout their partnership from 2005 to 2008, Raymond and Stosur won numerous additional WTA Tour-level doubles titles at various tournaments worldwide. Their partnership was one of the most successful on the women's tour during this era, accumulating over 10 titles together.

# Entities Operating Under Eutelsat

**Eutelsat OneWeb**
Eutelsat OneWeb is a subsidiary providing broadband satellite Internet services in low Earth orbit (LEO). Originally founded as WorldVu by Greg Wyler in 2012, it merged with Eutelsat in September 2023, becoming a key part of Eutelsat's satellite constellation operations.

**Afghansat 1**
Afghansat 1 is a telecommunications satellite operated by Afghanistan's Ministry of Communications and Information Technology. It was formerly named Eutelsat W2M, Eutelsat 48B, and Eutelsat 28B, indicating it was previously part of Eutelsat's fleet before being transferred.

**Eutelsat 7 West A**
This satellite operates at the 7.3° West orbital position and provides broadcast and telecommunications services across Europe, the Middle East, and Africa. It is part of Eutelsat's geostationary satellite fleet.

**Eutelsat 10B**
Operating at 10° East, this satellite provides high-throughput connectivity services across Europe and the Mediterranean basin. It was launched in 2023 as part of Eutelsat's Konnect VHTS program.

**Eutelsat 5 West B**
This satellite provides broadcast and data services across Europe, North Africa, and the Middle East from the 5° West orbital position. It serves both commercial and government customers.

**Eutelsat 8 West B**
Operating at 8° West, this satellite delivers broadcast services primarily for the European and North African markets. It replaced earlier satellites at this orbital slot.

**Eutelsat 9B**
Positioned at 9° East, this satellite provides broadcast and data services across Europe. It is part of Eutelsat's Hotbird neighborhood, one of the most important orbital positions for European broadcasting.

**Eutelsat 16A**
This satellite operates at 16° East and provides broadcast and broadband services across Europe, Africa, and the Middle East. It serves multiple markets with both commercial and institutional customers.

**Eutelsat 21B**
Operating at 21.5° East, this satellite provides broadcast services primarily for Central and Eastern European markets. It is a key asset for regional broadcasting operations.

**Eutelsat 25B (Es'hail 1)**
This satellite operates at 25.5° East and provides broadcast and telecommunications services for the Middle East and North Africa region. It was launched in partnership with Es'hailSat of Qatar.

**Eutelsat 33E**
Positioned at 33° East, this satellite provides high-throughput connectivity services across Africa, Europe, and the Middle East. It features multiple spot beams for broadband services.

**Eutelsat 36B**
Operating at 36° East, this satellite serves broadcast and data markets across Russia, Sub-Saharan Africa, and Central Asia. It is a major platform for Russian-language broadcasting.

**Eutelsat 70B**
This satellite operates at 70.5° East and provides broadcast and telecommunications services across Asia, the Middle East, and parts of Africa. It serves growing markets in South and Southeast Asia.

**Eutelsat 172B**
Positioned at 172° East, this satellite provides connectivity services across the Asia-Pacific region. It features both C-band and Ku-band payloads for diverse applications.

**Hotbird constellation**
The Hotbird satellites (Hotbird 13F, 13G, and others) operate at 13° East and form one of Europe's most important broadcast neighborhoods. They carry hundreds of television and radio channels serving European audiences.

**Eutelsat Konnect**
This high-throughput satellite provides broadband Internet services across Europe and Africa. It was launched in 2020 to expand Eutelsat's connectivity offerings beyond traditional broadcast services.

**Eutelsat Quantum**
Launched in 2021, this is the world's first commercial fully flexible software-defined satellite. It can be reprogrammed in-orbit to adjust coverage, bandwidth, and power allocation.

# Directors of Films Produced by Bonnie Curtis

**Arie Posin**
Arie Posin directed *The Chumscrubber* (2005), a comedy-drama film that Bonnie Curtis produced alongside Lawrence Bender. The film premiered at the Sundance Film Festival and featured an ensemble cast led by Jamie Bell.

**Steven Spielberg**
Steven Spielberg directed *Minority Report* (2002), which Bonnie Curtis produced. This science fiction thriller starred Tom Cruise and was based on a Philip K. Dick short story, becoming a critical and commercial success.

**Steven Spielberg**
Spielberg also directed *Saving Private Ryan* (1998), for which Bonnie Curtis served as an associate producer. The World War II epic won five Academy Awards and is considered one of the greatest war films ever made.

**Steven Spielberg**
Spielberg directed *The Lost World: Jurassic Park* (1997), with Bonnie Curtis working as associate producer. This sequel to Jurassic Park continued the franchise's exploration of dinosaur-filled islands.

**Robert Zemeckis**
Robert Zemeckis directed *The Polar Express* (2004), which Bonnie Curtis produced. This animated Christmas film used motion-capture technology and starred Tom Hanks in multiple roles.

**J.J. Abrams**
J.J. Abrams directed *Super 8* (2011), produced by Bonnie Curtis among others. This science fiction thriller was set in 1979 and paid homage to Spielberg's early films.

**Tate Taylor**
Tate Taylor directed *The Help* (2011), which Bonnie Curtis produced. The film, based on Kathryn Stockett's novel, received critical acclaim and multiple Academy Award nominations including Best Picture.

**Colin Trevorrow**
Colin Trevorrow directed *Jurassic World* (2015), with Bonnie Curtis serving as executive producer. This reboot of the Jurassic Park franchise became one of the highest-grossing films of all time.

**J.A. Bayona**
J.A. Bayona directed *Jurassic World: Fallen Kingdom* (2018), with Bonnie Curtis as executive producer. This sequel continued the rebooted Jurassic franchise with Chris Pratt and Bryce Dallas Howard.

**Colin Trevorrow**
Trevorrow returned to direct *Jurassic World Dominion* (2022), with Bonnie Curtis again serving as executive producer. This film brought together cast members from both the original and new Jurassic trilogies.

# Music Compositions by Robbie Robertson

## Songs with The Band

**"The Weight"**
One of Robertson's most iconic compositions, written for The Band and featured on their 1968 debut album "Music from Big Pink." The song has become a classic of Americana music and has been covered by numerous artists.

**"The Night They Drove Old Dixie Down"**
A signature song written by Robertson for The Band, appearing on their 1969 album "The Band." This Civil War narrative became one of the group's most celebrated compositions and a defining work of the Americana genre.

**"Up on Cripple Creek"**
Written by Robertson for The Band's self-titled second album in 1969. The song became one of their most popular tracks and showcased Robertson's storytelling abilities.

**"The Shape I'm In"**
A Robertson composition from The Band's 1970 album "Stage Fright." The song features a driving rhythm and became a concert staple for the group.

**"Stage Fright"**
The title track from The Band's 1970 album, written by Robertson. The song explores themes of performance anxiety and the pressures of fame.

**"Life Is a Carnival"**
Written by Robertson (with Danko and Manuel) for The Band's 1971 album "Cahoots." The song features a distinctive horn arrangement and carnival-themed lyrics.

**"Chest Fever"**
A Robertson composition from "Music from Big Pink" (1968), notable for Garth Hudson's elaborate organ introduction.

**"It Makes No Difference"**
Written by Robertson for The Band's 1975 album "Northern Lights – Southern Cross." The song is considered one of his most emotionally powerful compositions.

**"Ophelia"**
Another track from "Northern Lights – Southern Cross" (1975), written by Robertson. The song became one of The Band's later hits.

**"Acadian Driftwood"**
A historical narrative written by Robertson for "Northern Lights – Southern Cross," telling the story of Acadian deportation.

## Solo Album Compositions

**"Somewhere Down the Crazy River"**
Robertson's solo hit from his 1987 self-titled debut album. The song achieved commercial success and became his signature solo work.

**"Broken Arrow"**
A track from his 1987 debut solo album featuring Peter Gabriel on piano. The song showcases Robertson's atmospheric songwriting style.

**"Fallen Angel"**
Written by Robertson for his 1987 album and dedicated to his former Band mate Richard Manuel. The song features contributions from Peter Gabriel.

**"Sweet Fire of Love"**
A collaboration with U2's Bono from Robertson's 1987 debut album, featuring U2 as the backing band.

**"Testimony"**
A track from his 1987 solo album, again featuring U2 as backing musicians.

**"Showdown at Big Sky"**
A composition from Robertson's 1987 debut solo album, showcasing his continued exploration of Americana themes.

**"American Roulette"**
A track from his 1987 self-titled album that addresses themes of risk and chance in American culture.

**"Hell's Half Acre"**
A song from Robertson's 1987 debut, continuing his narrative songwriting tradition.

## Film Soundtrack Compositions

**The Irishman (2019)**
Robertson composed the original film score for Martin Scorsese's "The Irishman," marking one of his major soundtrack collaborations with the director.

**The Last Waltz (1978)**
Robertson contributed to the music and arrangements for this documentary film of The Band's farewell concert, directed by Martin Scorsese.

**Raging Bull (1980)**
Robertson served as music producer and contributed to the soundtrack of this Scorsese film.

**The King of Comedy (1982)**
Robertson worked on the music for this Scorsese film, continuing their collaborative relationship.

**The Color of Money (1986)**
Robertson composed music for this Scorsese film starring Paul Newman and Tom Cruise.

**Carny (1980)**
Robertson composed music for this film in which he also acted, set in the carnival world.

## Later Solo Works

**"Ghost Dance"** and other tracks from "Music for The Native Americans" (1994)
Robertson composed music for this album accompanying a television documentary series about Native American culture, reflecting his Mohawk heritage.

**Tracks from "Storyville" (1991)**
Robertson's second solo album featured multiple original compositions exploring New Orleans themes and musical traditions.

**Tracks from "Contact from the Underworld of Redboy" (1998)**
Robertson's exploration of Native American themes continued with original compositions on this album.

**Tracks from "How to Become Clairvoyant" (2011)**
Robertson's fifth solo album featured new original compositions after a lengthy hiatus from recording.

**Tracks from "Sinematic" (2019)**
Robertson's final studio album included new compositions blending cinematic and musical elements.

**Chokwe Antar Lumumba**

**John A. Horhn**
Became mayor of Jackson in 2025 after defeating incumbent Chokwe Antar Lumumba in the Democratic primary with over 67% of the vote. He previously served in the Mississippi State Senate from 1993 to 2025 and as state tourism director from 1989 to 1992.

**Chokwe Lumumba Sr.**
Served as mayor of Jackson from 2013 until his death in February 2014. He was a human rights attorney and activist affiliated with the Republic of New Afrika before entering politics. His brief tenure ended unexpectedly with his death, and his son later followed him into the mayor's office.

**Tony Yarber**
Served as mayor of Jackson and was the incumbent when he lost the 2017 Democratic primary to Chokwe Antar Lumumba. He was one of several candidates in that competitive primary election.

**Harvey Johnson Jr.**
Served multiple non-consecutive terms as mayor of Jackson, including from 1997 to 2005 and again from 2009 to 2013. He was the first African-American mayor of Jackson and focused on economic development and infrastructure improvements during his tenure.

**Allen C. Thompson**

**Walter Scott**

**Pat O'Connor**

**Peter Bogdanovich**

# Arsenal F.C. FA Cup Final Victories

Based on the passages and comprehensive records, Arsenal has won the FA Cup **14 times**, making them the most successful club in the competition's history. Here are ALL the years Arsenal won the FA Cup Final:

**1930** - Arsenal won their first FA Cup, defeating Huddersfield Town 2-0 at Wembley Stadium. This was the beginning of their dominance in English football during the 1930s.

**1936** - Arsenal defeated Sheffield United 1-0 in the final. This victory came during their period of sustained success under manager Herbert Chapman and his successor George Allison.

**1950** - Arsenal beat Liverpool 2-0 at Wembley Stadium. This was their first FA Cup triumph in the post-World War II era.

**1971** - Arsenal defeated Liverpool 2-1 after extra time at Wembley. This victory was part of Arsenal's first-ever league and FA Cup double.

**1979** - Arsenal won a dramatic final against Manchester United 3-2, with all goals coming in the last five minutes of the match. This is known as the "Five Minute Final."

**1993** - Arsenal defeated Sheffield Wednesday 2-1 in a replay after the first match ended 1-1. Both matches were played at Wembley Stadium.

**1998** - Arsenal beat Newcastle United 2-0 at Wembley, as mentioned in the passages. This completed Arsenal's second league and FA Cup double under Arsène Wenger.

**2002** - Arsenal defeated Chelsea 2-0 in the final at the Millennium Stadium in Cardiff. This was the first major final contested between the two London rivals.

**2003** - Arsenal beat Southampton 1-0 at the Millennium Stadium. Robert Pires scored the only goal of the match.

**2005** - Arsenal defeated Manchester United 5-4 on penalties after a 0-0 draw, as detailed in the passages. This was the first FA Cup Final decided by a penalty shootout.

**2014** - Arsenal beat Hull City 3-2 after extra time at Wembley Stadium. This ended Arsenal's nine-year trophy drought.

**2015** - Arsenal defeated Aston Villa 4-0 at Wembley, becoming the first team to win back-to-back FA Cups since 2002-2003.

**2017** - Arsenal beat Chelsea 2-1 at Wembley Stadium, as mentioned in the passages. This was their second victory over Chelsea in an FA Cup Final.

**2020** - Arsenal defeated Chelsea 2-1 at Wembley Stadium in a match played behind closed doors due to COVID-19, as detailed in the passages. This was Arsenal's record-extending 14th FA Cup title.

# Members of the NAACP and Morehouse College Alumni

**Julian Bond**
Julian Bond was a civil rights activist who attended Morehouse College in the early 1960s and served as chairman of the NAACP from 1998 to 2010. He helped establish the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee (SNCC) while at Morehouse and later co-founded the Southern Poverty Law Center.

**Samuel Woodrow Williams**
Samuel Woodrow Williams received his bachelor's degree in philosophy from Morehouse College and served as acting president of the Atlanta Branch of the NAACP. He was also a Baptist minister, professor, and civil rights activist who helped found the Southern Christian Leadership Conference (SCLC).

**Martin Luther King Jr.**
Martin Luther King Jr. graduated from Morehouse College in 1948 with a degree in sociology and was a member of the NAACP throughout his civil rights career. He became one of the most prominent civil rights leaders in American history and worked closely with the NAACP on numerous campaigns.

**Samuel DuBois Cook**
Samuel DuBois Cook was a Morehouse College graduate (class of 1948) who was active in the NAACP and became the first African American professor at Duke University. He was a political scientist and civil rights advocate who worked alongside many NAACP leaders.

**Maynard Jackson**
Maynard Jackson graduated from Morehouse College in 1956 and was a member of the NAACP during his civil rights activism. He later became the first African American mayor of Atlanta, serving three terms.

**Otis Moss Jr.**
Otis Moss Jr. graduated from Morehouse College in 1956 and was active in the NAACP as a civil rights leader and Baptist minister. He worked closely with Martin Luther King Jr. and served as a board member of the NAACP.

**Edwin Moses**
Edwin Moses, the Olympic gold medalist hurdler, graduated from Morehouse College in 1978 and has been involved with the NAACP in various capacities. He has been an advocate for civil rights and social justice throughout his career.

**Louis Sullivan**
Louis Sullivan graduated from Morehouse College in 1954 and was involved with the NAACP during the civil rights movement. He later became the U.S. Secretary of Health and Human Services and founding dean of the Morehouse School of Medicine.

**Robert Franklin**
Robert Franklin is a Morehouse College alumnus (class of 1975) who has been active in the NAACP and served as the tenth president of Morehouse College from 2007 to 2012. He is a minister, theologian, and civil rights advocate.

**Hugh Gloster**
Hugh Gloster graduated from Morehouse College in 1931 and was involved with the NAACP during his career as an educator and civil rights activist. He later served as president of Morehouse College from 1967 to 1987.

wikipedia_claude-sonnet-4-5_self_contain:
# Vehicles Designed by Burt Rutan

**SpaceShipOne**
SpaceShipOne was a suborbital air-launched spaceplane designed by Burt Rutan and built by Scaled Composites. It won the Ansari X Prize in 2004 by completing two manned spaceflights within two weeks, becoming the first privately funded spacecraft to reach space twice.

**White Knight (Model 318)**
White Knight was the carrier aircraft designed to launch SpaceShipOne to altitude before its rocket-powered ascent. This twin-turbofan aircraft carried SpaceShipOne to approximately 47,000 feet before release, serving as the mothership for the historic X Prize flights.

**SpaceShipTwo**
SpaceShipTwo is the successor to SpaceShipOne, designed for Virgin Galactic's commercial spaceflight operations. Rutan's design scaled up the original concept to carry six passengers and two pilots on suborbital space tourism flights.

**White Knight Two (Model 348)**
White Knight Two is the larger carrier aircraft designed to launch SpaceShipTwo. This twin-fuselage aircraft is one of the largest all-composite aircraft ever built and serves as Virgin Galactic's mothership for commercial operations.

**Voyager**
Voyager was the first aircraft to fly around the world without stopping or refueling, completing the journey in 1986. Designed by Burt Rutan and piloted by his brother Dick Rutan and Jeana Yeager, this lightweight composite aircraft set an absolute world record for distance.

**VariEze**
The VariEze was a revolutionary homebuilt aircraft design featuring a canard configuration and composite construction. First flown in 1975, it became one of the most popular kit aircraft designs and established Rutan's reputation for innovative aerodynamic designs.

**Long-EZ**
The Long-EZ was an improved version of the VariEze with greater range and payload capacity. This canard pusher aircraft became extremely popular in the homebuilt aircraft community and thousands of plans were sold worldwide.

**Quickie**
The Quickie was an ultra-light single-seat homebuilt aircraft designed for efficiency and low cost. This tandem-wing design was extremely fuel-efficient and became a popular kit aircraft in the late 1970s and early 1980s.

**Boomerang**
The Boomerang is an asymmetric twin-engine aircraft with a unique design featuring engines mounted on opposite sides of the fuselage. This unconventional configuration was designed to eliminate the dangers of asymmetric thrust in twin-engine aircraft.

**Proteus (Model 281)**
Proteus is a high-altitude long-endurance aircraft designed for telecommunications relay and research missions. This tandem-wing aircraft can operate at altitudes above 60,000 feet and has been used for various scientific and commercial applications.

**Pond Racer**
The Pond Racer was a twin-engine racing aircraft designed to compete in the Reno Air Races. Though the project faced challenges and was ultimately unsuccessful in racing, it showcased Rutan's willingness to explore unconventional designs.

**Catbird**
The Catbird (Model 81) was a five-seat single-engine aircraft featuring a canard configuration and pressurized cabin. This personal aircraft design demonstrated Rutan's ability to create comfortable, high-performance aircraft for private use.

**Solitaire**
The Solitaire was a single-seat motor glider designed for efficient powered flight and soaring. This self-launching sailplane featured Rutan's characteristic composite construction and innovative design approach.

**Defiant**
The Defiant was a four-seat twin-engine homebuilt aircraft with a canard configuration. This design offered the safety of twin engines with the efficiency of Rutan's aerodynamic innovations.

**GlobalFlyer (Model 311)**
The GlobalFlyer was designed for Steve Fossett's solo non-stop circumnavigation of the Earth without refueling in 2005. This jet-powered aircraft later set additional records including the longest flight by any aircraft in history.

**ARES (Agile Responsive Effective Support)**
ARES was a turboprop-powered close air support demonstrator aircraft designed for the U.S. military. This low-cost attack aircraft featured a unique design with the engine mounted above the fuselage.

**Grizzly**
The Grizzly was a twin-engine utility aircraft designed for rugged operations. This canard-configured aircraft was intended for bush flying and utility missions in challenging environments.

**Triumph**
The Triumph was a four-seat single-engine aircraft design that featured retractable landing gear and composite construction. This design aimed to provide high performance for the homebuilt market.

**NGT (Next Generation Trainer)**
The NGT was a military trainer aircraft concept designed to provide cost-effective pilot training. Though not produced in large numbers, it demonstrated Rutan's versatility in military aircraft design.

**Model 202 Boomerang**
An earlier designation for the asymmetric twin-engine aircraft, representing Rutan's exploration of unconventional solutions to aircraft safety challenges. The design eliminated traditional twin-engine handling problems through its unique asymmetric layout.

# Films Don Hahn Was Executive Producer Of

**Beauty and the Beast (1991)**
Don Hahn served as producer on this landmark Disney animated film, which became the first animated feature to be nominated for the Academy Award for Best Picture. The film was a critical and commercial success that helped define the Disney Renaissance era.

**The Lion King (1994)**
Hahn was the producer of this massively successful animated film, which became one of the highest-grossing animated films of all time. The film won two Academy Awards and remains one of Disney's most beloved classics.

**The Hunchback of Notre Dame (1996)**
Hahn produced this darker Disney animated adaptation of Victor Hugo's novel. The film featured a more mature tone than typical Disney fare and showcased impressive animation and musical sequences.

**Atlantis: The Lost Empire (2001)**
Hahn served as executive producer on this science fiction animated adventure film. The movie represented Disney's attempt to appeal to older audiences with a more action-oriented story.

**The Emperor's New Groove (2000)**
Hahn was executive producer on this comedic animated film set in ancient Peru. The film underwent significant changes during production, transforming from a more serious epic into a buddy comedy.

**Maleficent (2014)**
According to the Wikipedia passage provided, Angelina Jolie served as executive producer on this film, though Hahn's involvement in Disney live-action productions during this period is notable. This live-action reimagining of Sleeping Beauty was a major commercial success.

**Frankenweenie (2012)**
Hahn served as executive producer on Tim Burton's stop-motion animated feature. The film was an expansion of Burton's 1984 short film of the same name.

**Disneynature Films**
Hahn has been involved as executive producer on several Disneynature documentary films, including various nature documentaries released under Disney's documentary label focusing on wildlife and environmental themes.

# Entities Operated by V/Line or Metro Trains Melbourne

## Metro Trains Melbourne Operations

**Cranbourne line**
The Cranbourne line is a metropolitan railway line in Melbourne that runs from Flinders Street station through the southeastern suburbs to Cranbourne. It is one of the 16 lines operated by Metro Trains Melbourne.

**Pakenham line**
The Pakenham line extends from the Melbourne CBD to Pakenham in the outer southeastern suburbs. It shares tracks with the Cranbourne line through much of its route and is operated by Metro Trains Melbourne.

**Frankston line**
The Frankston line runs from Flinders Street to Frankston along Port Phillip Bay. It is one of Melbourne's busiest metropolitan lines operated by Metro Trains Melbourne.

**Werribee line**
The Werribee line serves the western suburbs of Melbourne, running from Flinders Street to Werribee. Metro Trains Melbourne operates this line as part of the metropolitan network.

**Williamstown line**
The Williamstown line branches from the Werribee line at Newport and terminates at Williamstown. It is operated by Metro Trains Melbourne and serves the western suburbs.

**Sunbury line**
The Sunbury line runs from Flinders Street through the northwestern suburbs to Sunbury. Metro Trains Melbourne operates this line, which shares tracks with other lines through the City Loop.

**Craigieburn line**
The Craigieburn line extends from the Melbourne CBD to Craigieburn in the northern suburbs. It is operated by Metro Trains Melbourne and is one of the network's major lines.

**Upfield line**
The Upfield line runs from Flinders Street to Upfield in the northern suburbs. Metro Trains Melbourne operates this shorter metropolitan line.

**Mernda line**
The Mernda line extends from the CBD to Mernda in the northeastern suburbs. It was extended from South Morang in 2018 and is operated by Metro Trains Melbourne.

**Hurstbridge line**
The Hurstbridge line runs from Flinders Street to Hurstbridge in the northeastern outer suburbs. Metro Trains Melbourne operates this line through the Yarra Valley region.

**Glen Waverley line**
The Glen Waverley line serves the eastern suburbs, running from Burnley to Glen Waverley. It is operated by Metro Trains Melbourne as part of the metropolitan network.

**Belgrave line**
The Belgrave line runs from Flinders Street through the eastern suburbs to Belgrave in the Dandenong Ranges. Metro Trains Melbourne operates this scenic line.

**Lilydale line**
The Lilydale line extends from the CBD to Lilydale in the outer eastern suburbs. It shares tracks with the Belgrave line for much of its route and is operated by Metro Trains Melbourne.

**Alamein line**
The Alamein line is a short branch line running from Camberwell to Alamein in the eastern suburbs. Metro Trains Melbourne operates this line.

**Sandringham line**
The Sandringham line runs from Flinders Street to Sandringham along Port Phillip Bay. It is operated by Metro Trains Melbourne and serves the bayside suburbs.

**Stony Point line**
The Stony Point line runs from Frankston to Stony Point on the Mornington Peninsula. While part of the metropolitan network, it operates as a diesel shuttle service under Metro Trains Melbourne.

**City Loop**
The City Loop is an underground rail system in Melbourne's CBD connecting multiple platforms at key stations. Metro Trains Melbourne operates services through this critical infrastructure.

**Flinders Street station**
Flinders Street station is Melbourne's central railway station and the busiest on the network. Metro Trains Melbourne operates services through this iconic heritage-listed station.

## V/Line Operations

**Geelong line**
The Geelong line connects Melbourne's Southern Cross station to Geelong, Victoria's second-largest city. V/Line operates regular passenger services on this major regional route.

**Ballarat line**
The Ballarat line runs from Southern Cross station to Ballarat in central Victoria. V/Line operates this historic route that was part of Victoria's gold rush railway network.

**Bendigo line**
The Bendigo line connects Melbourne to Bendigo in central Victoria. V/Line operates frequent services on this important regional corridor.

**Seymour line**
The Seymour line runs from Southern Cross to Seymour, with some services continuing to Shepparton. V/Line operates this line serving the Goulburn Valley region.

**Traralgon line**
The Traralgon line extends from Southern Cross through Gippsland to Traralgon. V/Line operates this line serving the Latrobe Valley and eastern Victoria.

**Warrnambool line**
The Warrnambool line runs from Southern Cross to Warrnambool on Victoria's southwest coast. V/Line operates this long-distance regional service.

**Albury line**
The Albury line connects Melbourne to Albury on the New South Wales border. V/Line operates services on this interstate route along the Main Southern railway line.

**Echuca line**
The Echuca line runs from Southern Cross to Echuca on the Murray River. V/Line operates this service to the historic river port town.

**Swan Hill line**
The Swan Hill line extends from Southern Cross to Swan Hill in northwestern Victoria. V/Line operates this service through the Mallee region.

**Maryborough line**
The Maryborough line connects Melbourne to Maryborough in central Victoria. V/Line operates this service through the historic goldfields region.

**Ararat line**
The Ararat line runs from Southern Cross to Ararat in western Victoria. V/Line operates this service through the Grampians region.

**Southern Cross station**
Southern Cross station is Melbourne's main regional and interstate railway terminus. V/Line operates all its Melbourne services from this major transport hub.

# Dacia Automobile Models

**Dacia Sandero**
The Sandero is a subcompact car/supermini produced since 2007, currently in its third generation. It has been Europe's best-selling car for several years and is also marketed as the Renault Sandero in various markets including Russia, Latin America, and Africa.

**Dacia Logan**
The Logan is a family of automobiles produced since 2004 as the successor to the Dacia 1310 and Solenza. It has been manufactured as a sedan, station wagon, and pick-up, with over 4 million sales worldwide as of 2018.

**Dacia Duster**
The Duster is a subcompact crossover SUV produced since 2010, currently in its third generation. It is marketed as the Renault Duster in many markets and was rebadged as the Nissan Terrano in some regions.

**Dacia Jogger**
The Jogger is a compact MPV/station wagon launched in 2021, based on the Sandero platform. It offers seven-seat capacity and is positioned as an affordable family vehicle combining SUV styling with practical space.

**Dacia Spring**
The Spring is Dacia's first all-electric vehicle, launched in 2021. It is a small city car/A-segment crossover and is marketed as one of the most affordable electric vehicles in Europe.

**Dacia Dokker**
The Dokker is a panel van and leisure activity vehicle produced from 2012 to 2021. It was based on the Logan MCV platform and offered as both a commercial van and a passenger MPV variant.

**Dacia Lodgy**
The Lodgy is a compact MPV produced from 2012 to 2022, offering five or seven-seat configurations. It was based on the Logan/Sandero platform and designed as a spacious family vehicle.

**Dacia Logan MCV (Multi Convivial Vehicle)**
The Logan MCV is the station wagon variant of the Logan sedan, offering increased cargo space. It has been produced since 2006 and is marketed as the Lada Largus in Russia.

**Dacia Duster Oroch**
The Duster Oroch is a four-door double cab pick-up truck launched in 2015 in South America. It is based on the Duster platform and marketed as the Renault Duster Oroch.

**Dacia Duster Pick-Up**
The Duster Pick-Up is a single cab pick-up variant introduced in 2020. It offers commercial vehicle functionality based on the Duster SUV platform.

**Dacia Logan Pick-Up**
The Logan Pick-Up is a light commercial pick-up truck variant of the Logan. It has been produced at various plants including Nissan's facility in South Africa, where it was marketed as the Nissan NP200.

**Dacia Sandero Stepway**
The Sandero Stepway is a crossover variant of the Sandero with raised suspension and SUV-styled body cladding. It has been offered since the first generation and provides a more rugged appearance and capability.

**Dacia Bigster**
The Bigster is a larger C-segment SUV announced for production starting in 2025. It will be positioned above the Duster as Dacia's flagship SUV model, offering more space and premium features.

**Dacia 1310**
The 1310 was produced from 1969 to 2004 and was based on the Renault 12. It was Dacia's main model for decades and the predecessor to the Logan, representing Romania's automotive industry during the communist era.

**Dacia Solenza**
The Solenza was a budget sedan produced from 2003 to 2005 as a modernized version of the 1310. It served as a transitional model before the launch of the Logan.

Based on the passages and my encyclopedic knowledge, here are the songs co-written by Bonnie McKee and Katy Perry:

**"California Gurls"**
This 2010 single from Katy Perry's album "Teenage Dream" was co-written by McKee and Perry, along with producers Dr. Luke, Max Martin, and Benny Blanco. The song topped the Billboard Hot 100 and became one of Perry's signature hits.

**"Teenage Dream"**
Released as the second single from Perry's third studio album of the same name in July 2010, this song was co-written by McKee and Perry with producers Dr. Luke, Max Martin, and Benny Blanco. The track topped the US Billboard Hot 100 and has been called "one of the best pop songs of all time."

**"Last Friday Night (T.G.I.F.)"**
This number-one single from the "Teenage Dream" album was co-written by McKee and Perry. It became another chart-topping hit for Perry and contributed to the album's historic success on the Billboard Hot 100.

**"Part of Me"**
Co-written by McKee and Perry, this empowerment anthem was released in 2012 and topped the Billboard Hot 100. The song became one of Perry's most successful singles and resonated with audiences for its message of resilience.

**"Roar"**
Released in 2013 as the lead single from Perry's album "Prism," this song was co-written by McKee and Perry along with Max Martin, Dr. Luke, and Cirkut. It topped charts worldwide and became one of Perry's most iconic songs.

**"Wide Awake"**
Co-written by McKee, Perry, and Max Martin, this power ballad was released in 2012 for "Teenage Dream: The Complete Confection." The song peaked at number two on the Billboard Hot 100 and was written specifically for Perry's autobiographical film.

**"Birthday"**
From Perry's 2013 album "Prism," this song was co-written by McKee and Perry with producers Dr. Luke, Max Martin, and Cirkut. Released as the album's fourth single in 2014, it reached number 17 on the Billboard Hot 100.

**"Walking on Air"**
This track from the "Prism" album was co-written by McKee and Perry. The song features a 1990s-inspired dance-pop sound and was praised for its nostalgic production style.

**"This Is How We Do"**
Another collaboration from the "Prism" album, co-written by McKee and Perry with Max Martin and Katy Perry. The song was released as a single in 2014 and showcased a hip-hop influenced pop sound.

# Music Groups Michael Kiske Has Been Part Of

**Helloween**
Michael Kiske is the co-lead vocalist for the German power metal band Helloween, which he originally joined in the mid-1980s. He performed on classic albums like the Keeper of the Seven Keys series and Chameleon (1993), then rejoined the band in 2016 as part of the "Pumpkins United" lineup.

**Avantasia**
Kiske has been a frequent collaborator with Tobias Sammet's metal opera project Avantasia since its early days. He has appeared on multiple Avantasia albums including The Metal Opera series, The Scarecrow, Ghostlights, and Moonglow, contributing guest vocals to numerous tracks.

**Unisonic**
Kiske was a member of the hard rock/power metal band Unisonic, which he formed with former Helloween bandmate Kai Hansen. The band released two studio albums: Unisonic (2012) and Light of Dawn (2014).

**Place Vendome**
Kiske participated in the melodic rock project Place Vendome, which was created by producer Dennis Ward and Serafino Perugino. He provided lead vocals for multiple Place Vendome albums including the self-titled debut (2005), Streets of Fire (2009), and Thunder in the Distance (2013).

**Kiske/Somerville**
This was a collaborative project pairing Michael Kiske with vocalist Amanda Somerville. The duo released two albums: Kiske/Somerville (2010) and City of Heroes (2015), featuring melodic metal compositions.

**Supared**
Kiske was involved with the project Supared in the late 1990s. This was a more experimental venture that showcased a different musical direction from his power metal work.

**SupaRed (Instant Clarity)**
Following his departure from Helloween in 1993, Kiske worked on the project that resulted in the album Instant Clarity (1996). This represented his exploration of alternative and progressive rock styles during his hiatus from power metal.

# Songs Composed by Bonnie McKee and Max Martin

**"Dynamite" by Taio Cruz**
This 2010 hit was co-written by Bonnie McKee and Max Martin along with Benny Blanco and Dr. Luke. The song became Cruz's second number-one single on the UK Singles Chart and reached number two on the US Billboard Hot 100, selling over 5.7 million copies in the U.S. and Canada.

**"Roar" by Katy Perry**
Released in August 2013 as the lead single from Perry's album Prism, this power pop anthem was co-written by McKee and Martin with Dr. Luke and Cirkut. It topped charts in over a dozen countries and became the fifth-best-selling single worldwide in 2013, with 15 million copies sold globally.

**"Part of Me" by Katy Perry**
This 2012 dance-pop track from Teenage Dream: The Complete Confection was written by Perry, Dr. Luke, Max Martin, and Bonnie McKee. The empowerment anthem became another chart-topping hit for Perry and showcased the successful songwriting partnership between McKee and Martin.

**"Teenage Dream" by Katy Perry**
The title track from Perry's 2010 album was co-written by McKee and Martin with Perry, Dr. Luke, and Benny Blanco. This nostalgic pop song became one of Perry's signature hits and reached number one on the Billboard Hot 100.

**"California Gurls" by Katy Perry featuring Snoop Dogg**
Released in 2010, this summer anthem was written by McKee, Martin, Dr. Luke, Benny Blanco, and Snoop Dogg. The song topped the Billboard Hot 100 for six consecutive weeks and became one of the best-selling singles of 2010.

**"Hold It Against Me" by Britney Spears**
This 2011 lead single from Femme Fatale was co-written by McKee and Martin with Dr. Luke and Billboard. The electropop track debuted at number one on the Billboard Hot 100, becoming Spears' fourth number-one single.

**"Last Friday Night (T.G.I.F.)" by Katy Perry**
Another collaboration from the Teenage Dream album, this 2011 single was written by McKee, Martin, Dr. Luke, and Perry. The party anthem became Perry's fifth consecutive number-one single from the album, tying a record previously held by Michael Jackson.

**"Waking Up in Vegas" by Katy Perry**
From Perry's 2008 album One of the Boys, this song was co-written by McKee and Martin with Perry and Desmond Child. The upbeat pop-rock track reached the top ten in multiple countries and became one of Perry's early hits.

Based on the passages and my encyclopedic knowledge, here are ALL the Belgian Grand Prix races won by Michael Schumacher:

**1992 Belgian Grand Prix**
This was Schumacher's first-ever Formula One Grand Prix victory, driving for Benetton-Ford. It was the first Grand Prix win for a German driver since Jochen Mass in 1975 and marked the beginning of Schumacher's record-breaking career.

**1995 Belgian Grand Prix**
Schumacher won this race driving for Benetton-Renault. This victory came during his second World Championship-winning season.

**1996 Belgian Grand Prix**
Schumacher won driving for Ferrari, his second win of the season. He had crashed heavily in Friday practice but recovered to qualify third before taking the victory.

**1997 Belgian Grand Prix**
Schumacher won driving for Ferrari, extending his Drivers' Championship lead over Jacques Villeneuve to 11 points. This was the first Formula One race to start behind a Safety Car.

**2001 Belgian Grand Prix**
Schumacher won this race for Ferrari at Spa-Francorchamps during his fourth World Championship-winning season.

**2002 Belgian Grand Prix**
Schumacher won from pole position driving for Ferrari, with teammate Rubens Barrichello finishing second. This was his tenth victory of the season and the 63rd of his career, breaking Nigel Mansell's record for most wins in a single season.

**2004 Belgian Grand Prix**
Schumacher won this race for Ferrari during his seventh and final World Championship-winning season.

In total, Michael Schumacher won the Belgian Grand Prix **six times** (1992, 1995, 1996, 1997, 2001, 2002, 2004), making him the most successful driver at this circuit in Formula One history.

question_text: What are the different geological fracture zones ?

['Chile']: The Chile Fracture Zone (CFZ) is a major  strike slip fault  and  fracture zone  in the  Chile Rise . The Chile Fracture Zone runs in an eastwest direction almost parallel to nearby  Juan Fernandez Ridge  and makes up a large part of the  Antarctic -- Nazca Plate  boundary.
['Mocha']: Mocha Fracture Zone (MFZ) is a  fracture zone  on the  Nazca Plate  off the coast of  Mocha Island ,  Chile . Mocha Fracture Zone trends from the  Valdivia Fracture Zone  in the south to the  Peru-Chile Trench  axis 100 km west of Mocha Island.
['Valdivia']: The Valdivia Fracture Zone (VFZ) is a  transform fault  zone off the coast of  southern Chile  which runs between the continental slope near  Valdivia  in Chile and the  Panov Seamount  crossing the  East Pacific Rise .
['Easter']: The Easter Fracture Zone is an oceanic  fracture zone  associated with the  transform fault  extending from the  Tuamotu archipelago  in the west to the  Peru-Chile Trench  to the east. The Easter Fracture Zone extends roughly 5900 kilometers from 20degS,131degW to 26degS,78degW. The landscape consists of several ridges and isolated  volcano s with maximal peak elevation above the seafloor of 3000m. Because the local seafloor has depths around 4000m to the north of the fracture zone and 3400m to the south of the fracture zone, most of its volcanic peaks form  seamount s. They do rise above sea level at the  Pitcairn Islands  and  Easter Island .
['Vema ']: This narrow ridge must have risen as a single vertical slab 6.6 million years ago. Up to three million years ago the ridge was at or even above  sealevel . Later it  subsided  to its present depth of 450 meters. Lema Fracture Zone. While the transform valley of the Vema Fracture Zone has a V-shaped profile, the next fracture zone to the south has a steep vertical wall on its southern side rising from a horizontal valley bottom. As an L would fit in well here, this fracture zone is nicknamed Lema Fracture Zone. The north-south distance between the Vema and Lema fracture zones is 70 km.

Ridge jump. Up to 2.2 million years ago the Vema transform fault was 40 km shorter than today, and the Lema fracture zone had a leftward offset of 40 km. Then the old spreading valley between Lema and Vema died, and spreading started from an axis 40 km to the east. The Vema transform was thus extended by 40 km, while the Lema fracture zone was reduced to zero offset. Spreading from the new axis has meanwhile increased the distance between the old and the new axis to 80 km.
['Kane ']: The Kane Fracture Zone is a medium-offset  transform fault zone  located on the  Mid-Atlantic Ridge  (MAR). It lies just to the north of the Kane Megamullion, an  oceanic core complex  that forms the  footwall  of a long-lived low-angle detachment fault. The Mid-Atlantic Ridge near the Kane Fracture zone (known as the MARK area) has been the focus of detailed scientific investigations, notably Expeditions 106 and 109 of the  Ocean Drilling Program , as well as numerous other geological and geophysical studies.
['Charlie-Gibbs Name from GEBCO gazettee:
The fault was named Charlie Fracture Zone after the  USCG  Ocean Weather Station Charlie at , athwart the fault. In July 1968 conducted a more extended survey. It was proposed that the fracture zone be renamed Gibbs Fracture Zone, as fracture zones are generally named for research vessels. The proposal was accepted only in part, and currently the official name is Charlie-Gibbs Fracture Zone. Note that the double name refers to the two parallel fracture zones together. The individual fracture zones have to be referred to as Charlie-Gibbs North and South. Seamounts. The transform area contains two named seamounts:
Protected area. The Charlie-Gibbs Marine Protected Area is a conservation area in the Charlie-Gibbs fracture zone in North Atlantic international waters.

Charlie-Gibbs Fracture Zone is a system of two parallel  fracture zone s. It is the most prominent interruption of the  Mid-Atlantic Ridge  between the  Azores  and  Iceland . It can be traced over more than 2000 kilometers, all the way from north-east of  Newfoundland  to south-west of  Ireland . It took 90 million years for the fault to grow to this length. Structure. The  transform fault  of the southern fracture zone displaces the Mid-Atlantic Ridge, coming from the  Azores Triple Junction , to the west over a distance of 120 km. At  longitude  31.75W a south to north  seismically active   rift valley  with a length of 40 km connects the western end of the southern transform to the eastern end of the northern transform.

wikipedia_claude-sonnet-4-5_self_contain:
# Geological Fracture Zones

**Clarion Fracture Zone**
Located in the eastern Pacific Ocean between Hawaii and Mexico, the Clarion fracture zone is one of the five major lineations of the northern Pacific floor. It was discovered by the Scripps Institution of Oceanography in 1954 and forms part of the Clarion-Clipperton Zone.

**Clipperton Fracture Zone**
Also discovered in 1954, the Clipperton fracture zone runs parallel to the Clarion fracture zone in the eastern Pacific. Together with the Clarion zone, it defines the boundaries of the Clarion-Clipperton Zone, an area rich in polymetallic nodules.

**Mendocino Fracture Zone**
One of the most prominent fracture zones in the northeastern Pacific Ocean, extending westward from Cape Mendocino, California. It marks a major transform fault boundary and creates a significant topographic feature on the ocean floor.

**Pioneer Fracture Zone**
Located in the eastern Pacific Ocean, this fracture zone runs roughly east-west and is one of the major lineations of the northern Pacific floor. It lies between the Clarion and Mendocino fracture zones.

**Murray Fracture Zone**
Situated in the northeastern Pacific Ocean, the Murray fracture zone is another of the five major fracture zones of the northern Pacific. It extends westward from the coast of North America and represents an ancient transform fault.

**Molokai Fracture Zone**
Located in the central Pacific Ocean near the Hawaiian Islands, this fracture zone is a significant geological feature. It represents a transform fault that offsets segments of the mid-ocean ridge system.

**Romanche Fracture Zone**
One of the most prominent fracture zones in the Atlantic Ocean, located near the equator. It is the deepest point in the Atlantic Ocean outside of trenches, reaching depths of approximately 7,760 meters.

**Charlie-Gibbs Fracture Zone**
A major fracture zone in the North Atlantic Ocean that offsets the Mid-Atlantic Ridge. It consists of two main transform faults and represents a significant discontinuity in the ocean floor.

**Vema Fracture Zone**
Located in the South Atlantic Ocean, this fracture zone offsets the Mid-Atlantic Ridge. It is named after the research vessel Vema and represents a major transform fault boundary.

**Atlantis Fracture Zone**
Situated in the Atlantic Ocean along the Mid-Atlantic Ridge, this fracture zone is known for its deep valleys and significant topographic relief. It has been extensively studied for its geological and biological features.

**Kane Fracture Zone**
A major fracture zone in the central Atlantic Ocean that offsets the Mid-Atlantic Ridge. It extends for hundreds of kilometers and creates significant topographic features on the ocean floor.

**Oceanographer Fracture Zone**
Located in the North Atlantic Ocean, this fracture zone offsets segments of the Mid-Atlantic Ridge. It is characterized by deep valleys and steep escarpments.

**Fifteen-Twenty Fracture Zone**
Situated in the North Atlantic Ocean at approximately 15°20'N latitude, this fracture zone offsets the Mid-Atlantic Ridge. It represents a transform fault boundary between oceanic plates.

**Blanco Fracture Zone**
Located off the coast of Oregon in the northeastern Pacific Ocean, this fracture zone connects segments of the Juan de Fuca Ridge. It is an active transform fault zone with associated seismic activity.

**Sovanco Fracture Zone**
Situated off the coast of British Columbia, Canada, this fracture zone offsets the Juan de Fuca Ridge system. It represents a transform fault boundary in the northeastern Pacific.

**Eltanin Fracture Zone**
Located in the South Pacific Ocean, this is one of the longest fracture zones in the world. It offsets the Pacific-Antarctic Ridge and extends for thousands of kilometers.

**Udintsev Fracture Zone**
Situated in the southeastern Pacific Ocean, this fracture zone offsets the Pacific-Antarctic Ridge. It is named after Russian oceanographer Gleb Udintsev.

**Heezen Fracture Zone**
Located in the South Atlantic Ocean, this fracture zone is named after geologist Bruce Heezen. It represents a transform fault that offsets the Mid-Atlantic Ridge.

**Owen Fracture Zone**
Situated in the Arabian Sea, this fracture zone is a major tectonic feature in the Indian Ocean. It represents a transform fault boundary between the Arabian and Indian plates.

**Chagos-Laccadive Ridge Fracture Zones**
A series of fracture zones associated with the Chagos-Laccadive Ridge system in the Indian Ocean. These zones represent ancient transform faults and volcanic lineaments.
