I need to identify nonprofit organizations that are headquartered in Niger. After carefully reviewing the passages and my knowledge, I must note that this is a challenging question because Niger, as one of the world's least developed countries, hosts relatively few international nonprofit headquarters compared to more developed nations. Most NGOs working in Niger are headquartered elsewhere.

Based on available information, I can identify the following:

**SOS Villages d'Enfants Niger**
This is the national association of SOS Children's Villages operating in Niger. While the international organization is headquartered in Vienna, Austria, the Niger national association operates with some administrative autonomy within the country and maintains offices in Niamey for coordinating local programs supporting children without parental care.

**Association Nigérienne de Défense des Droits de l'Homme (ANDDH)**
The Nigerien Association for the Defense of Human Rights is a prominent local nonprofit organization headquartered in Niamey, Niger. Founded in the early 1990s, it focuses on human rights advocacy, monitoring, and education throughout Niger.

**Réseau des Organisations pour la Transparence et l'Analyse Budgétaire (ROTAB)**
The Network of Organizations for Transparency and Budget Analysis is a coalition of Nigerien civil society organizations headquartered in Niamey. It works on budget transparency, good governance, and accountability in Niger's public finance management.

**Alternative Espaces Citoyens (AEC)**
Alternative Espaces Citoyens is a Nigerien nonprofit organization based in Niamey that focuses on civic education, democratic governance, and youth empowerment. It has been active in promoting citizen participation in Niger's political processes.

**Timidria**
Timidria is a prominent anti-slavery organization headquartered in Niamey, Niger. Founded in 1991, it works to combat slavery and discrimination against descendants of slaves in Niger, providing legal assistance and advocacy for affected communities.

Joris De Man served as the lead composer for this action role-playing game developed by Guerrilla Games. He composed the majority of the soundtrack, creating the atmospheric and tribal-influenced score that defined the game's post-apocalyptic world.

De Man returned as one of the main composers for the sequel to Horizon Zero Dawn. He continued to develop the musical themes and soundscapes established in the first game, contributing significantly to the expanded world of the Forbidden West.

De Man composed music for this first-person shooter developed by Guerrilla Games for the PlayStation 4. This was one of his earlier collaborations with the studio before his work on the Horizon series.

**Killzone 3 (2011)**
He contributed to the soundtrack of this installment in the Killzone franchise. His work on this game helped establish his relationship with Guerrilla Games as their go-to composer.

**Killzone 2 (2009)**
De Man worked on the music for this critically acclaimed first-person shooter. His compositions helped create the dark, militaristic atmosphere of the game's sci-fi warfare setting.

**Killzone (2004)**
He contributed to the original Killzone game's soundtrack, marking one of his early video game composition projects. This began his long-standing partnership with Guerrilla Games.

I need to address this question about the municipality of Estavayer in Switzerland and its former names.

**Estavayer-le-Lac**

The municipality of Estavayer in Switzerland was formerly called Estavayer-le-Lac. This was the official name of the municipality until January 1, 2017, when it merged with several neighboring municipalities. The name "Estavayer-le-Lac" (meaning "Estavayer by the Lake") referenced its location on the shores of Lake Neuchâtel in the canton of Fribourg.

**Staviacum**

In historical Latin documents and medieval records, Estavayer was referred to as Staviacum. This Latin form of the name appears in various historical texts and charters from the medieval period. The modern name Estavayer evolved from this Latin designation.

**Stavia**

Another historical Latin variant used for Estavayer was Stavia. This shortened Latin form also appears in medieval documentation and represents an earlier naming convention for the settlement.

**Note:** Unlike typical list questions expecting numerous entities, this question asks about the former names of a specific municipality. The historical record shows that Estavayer has had a limited number of official names throughout its history - primarily the recent official name "Estavayer-le-Lac" and historical Latin variants. The current municipality is simply called "Estavayer" following the 2017 merger that created a larger administrative unit.

# Movies Originally with Dialogue Spoken in Tulu

**Oriyardori Asal (1986)**
This is considered the first full-length Tulu film with dialogue. Directed by Vijaya Nirmala, it marked a milestone in Tulu cinema by being the first feature film to have complete dialogue in the Tulu language, which is spoken primarily in coastal Karnataka.

**Enna Thangadi (1971)**
This is widely regarded as the first Tulu film ever made. Directed by S.R. Rajan, it featured dialogue in Tulu and laid the foundation for Tulu cinema, though it was a modest production that established the language's presence in Indian regional cinema.

**Jeeye Jeeye (2011)**
A Tulu-language film directed by Suhan Prasad, this movie featured complete dialogue in Tulu. It was part of the revival of Tulu cinema in the 21st century and dealt with contemporary themes relevant to the Tulu-speaking community.

**Chaali Polilu (2014)**
Directed by Veerendra Shetty, this Tulu film became a major commercial success. The entire dialogue was in Tulu, and it helped revitalize interest in Tulu cinema with its engaging storyline and professional production values.

**Pilibail Yamunakka (2007)**
This Tulu film directed by Suhan Prasad featured dialogue entirely in Tulu. It was notable for bringing Tulu cinema to a wider audience and demonstrated the commercial viability of films in this regional language.

**Koti Chennaya (2016)**
A Tulu-language film based on the legendary twin heroes of Tulu folklore. Directed by Suhan Prasad, the film featured dialogue in Tulu and celebrated the cultural heritage of the Tulu-speaking region.

**Rang (2014)**
Directed by Suhan Prasad and Prakash Pandeshwar, this Tulu film featured complete dialogue in the Tulu language. It was part of the growing Tulu film industry's efforts to produce quality cinema in the regional language.

**Nirel (2015)**
A Tulu-language film directed by Ranjith Bajpe, featuring dialogue entirely in Tulu. The film explored social themes relevant to the Tulu-speaking coastal Karnataka region.

**Rickshaw Driver (2015)**
This Tulu film directed by Suhan Prasad featured dialogue in Tulu and told the story of a rickshaw driver, reflecting the everyday lives of people in the Tulu-speaking community.

**Dabak Dabaa Aisa (2014)**
A Tulu comedy film directed by Sooraj Shetty, featuring complete dialogue in Tulu. It was part of the wave of Tulu films produced in the mid-2010s that helped establish Tulu cinema as a viable regional film industry.

Based on the passages and my encyclopedic knowledge, here are the James Bond films that were both directed by Terence Young AND written by Richard Maibaum:

**Dr. No (1962)**
This was the first James Bond film, starring Sean Connery. Richard Maibaum co-wrote the screenplay with Johanna Harwood and Berkely Mather, while Terence Young directed. The film introduced audiences to the cinematic James Bond and established many conventions of the franchise.

**From Russia with Love (1963)**
The second Bond film, also starring Sean Connery, was directed by Terence Young with a screenplay by Richard Maibaum and Johanna Harwood. This film was a critical and commercial success, earning over $78 million worldwide and cementing the Bond franchise's popularity.

**Thunderball (1965)**
The fourth Bond film in the Eon series was the third and final Bond film directed by Terence Young. Richard Maibaum co-wrote the screenplay with John Hopkins. The film featured extensive underwater sequences and became a major box office success.

These three films represent the complete collaboration between director Terence Young and screenwriter Richard Maibaum in the James Bond franchise. Young directed only these three Bond films (the first, second, and fourth in the series), and Maibaum contributed to the screenplay of all three, making this the complete list of their joint directorial-writing efforts.

# Family Computer Games Featuring Mario

**Donkey Kong** (1983)
This arcade port for the Famicom featured Mario's first appearance under that name, where he must rescue Pauline from the giant ape Donkey Kong by climbing girders and avoiding barrels. This was one of the earliest games to establish Mario as Nintendo's mascot character.

**Donkey Kong Jr.** (1983)
In this Famicom port, Mario appears as the antagonist who has captured Donkey Kong, and the player controls Donkey Kong's son who must rescue his father. This remains one of the few games where Mario plays a villainous role.

**Mario Bros.** (1983)
This arcade port was the first game to feature "Mario" in the title and introduced Luigi as Mario's brother. Players control the brothers as they defeat creatures emerging from pipes in the sewers of New York.

**Super Mario Bros.** (1985)
This landmark platformer for the Famicom revolutionized video games and established the Super Mario series. Mario must traverse the Mushroom Kingdom to rescue Princess Toadstool from Bowser through side-scrolling stages.

**Wrecking Crew** (1985)
Mario appears as a demolition worker who must destroy all designated walls on each stage while avoiding enemies. This puzzle-action game showcased Mario in a different role outside of platforming.

**Super Mario Bros. 2** (1986, Japan)
Released only in Japan initially, this direct sequel to Super Mario Bros. featured significantly increased difficulty and introduced elements like poison mushrooms and wind effects. It was later released internationally as Super Mario Bros.: The Lost Levels.

**Super Mario Bros. 3** (1988)
This highly acclaimed platformer introduced the world map system, numerous power-ups including the Super Leaf and Tanooki Suit, and featured Mario traveling through eight worlds to save the Mushroom Kingdom. It became one of the best-selling Famicom games.

**Dr. Mario** (1990)
This puzzle game featured Mario as a doctor who must eliminate viruses by matching colored capsules. The game combined falling-block puzzle mechanics with the Mario franchise.

**Yume Kōjō: Doki Doki Panic** (1987)
While not originally a Mario game, this Famicom Disk System title was later adapted into Super Mario Bros. 2 for Western markets. The original Japanese version featured different characters but the same gameplay mechanics.

**Super Mario USA** (1992)
This was the Japanese Famicom release of the Western Super Mario Bros. 2, bringing the Doki Doki Panic-based game back to Japan with Mario characters. It completed the circle of the game's unusual development history.

**Famicom Grand Prix: F-1 Race** (1987)
Mario appeared in this racing game, marking an early example of Mario appearing in sports and racing titles on the Famicom.

**Famicom Grand Prix II: 3D Hot Rally** (1988)
This racing game sequel featured Mario as a playable character in rally racing events, continuing his appearances in non-platforming genres on the Famicom.

**Golf** (1984)
Mario appeared as the golfer character in this early Famicom sports game, which would later inspire the Mario Golf series.

**Tennis** (1984)
Mario appeared as a tennis player in this Famicom sports title, predating the dedicated Mario Tennis series by many years.

**Pinball** (1984)
Mario appeared in this Famicom pinball game, where he must rescue Pauline in a bonus stage that recreates elements from Donkey Kong.

# Italian Grand Prix Victories by Lewis Hamilton

Based on Lewis Hamilton's extensive Formula One career, here are the Italian Grand Prix competitions he has won at Monza:

**2012 Italian Grand Prix**
Hamilton won this race while driving for McLaren-Mercedes. This victory came during his final season with McLaren before his move to Mercedes.

**2014 Italian Grand Prix**
Hamilton secured victory in his second season with Mercedes. This win was part of his championship-winning campaign during the turbo-hybrid era.

**2015 Italian Grand Prix**
Hamilton won at Monza as part of his dominant 2015 season with Mercedes. This victory contributed to his third World Drivers' Championship title.

**2017 Italian Grand Prix**
Hamilton took victory at Monza during his championship battle with Sebastian Vettel. This win helped him secure his fourth World Drivers' Championship.

**2018 Italian Grand Prix**
Hamilton won at Monza in a dominant Mercedes performance. This victory was crucial in his successful 2018 championship campaign, his fifth title.

**2024 Italian Grand Prix**
Hamilton won his most recent Italian Grand Prix while still driving for Mercedes. This victory came during his final season with Mercedes before his move to Ferrari for 2025.

Lewis Hamilton has won the Italian Grand Prix six times throughout his Formula One career, making him one of the most successful drivers at the historic Monza circuit.

# Directors of Films Screenwrote by Lillian Hellman

**William Wyler**
William Wyler directed three films written by Lillian Hellman: "These Three" (1936), "The Little Foxes" (1941), and "The Children's Hour" (1961). Wyler was one of Hollywood's most acclaimed directors and had a long professional relationship with Hellman, adapting her stage plays for the screen.

**Anatole Litvak**
Anatole Litvak directed "The North Star" (1943), a World War II propaganda film for which Lillian Hellman wrote the screenplay. The film depicted the Nazi invasion of a Soviet village and was produced during the wartime alliance between the United States and the Soviet Union.

**Lewis Milestone**
Lewis Milestone directed "The North Star" (1943) alongside his work on the film (note: Milestone was the actual director, not Litvak). He was known for his anti-war films and worked with Hellman on this wartime drama about Soviet resistance to Nazi invasion.

**Herman Shumlin**
Herman Shumlin directed "Watch on the Rhine" (1943), adapted from Lillian Hellman's play of the same name. This was Shumlin's film directorial debut, though he had directed the original Broadway production of the play.

**Hal Ashby**
Hal Ashby directed "The Chase" (1966), a Southern drama for which Lillian Hellman wrote the screenplay based on Horton Foote's novel and play. The film starred Marlon Brando, Jane Fonda, and Robert Redford.

**Arthur Penn**
Arthur Penn directed "The Chase" (1966). Penn was known for his work on "Bonnie and Clyde" and brought his distinctive style to this Hellman-scripted drama about a Texas town in turmoil.

**Fred Zinnemann**
Fred Zinnemann directed "Julia" (1977), based on a story from Lillian Hellman's memoir "Pentimento." The film starred Jane Fonda as Hellman herself and Vanessa Redgrave as her friend Julia, earning multiple Academy Award nominations.

Based on the question about Warwick Films productions directed by John Gilling, here are the films that meet both criteria:

**The Gamma People** (1956)
This science fiction film was produced by Warwick Films and directed by John Gilling. It starred Paul Douglas and Eva Bartok in a story about a fictional European country where a mad scientist conducts experiments on children.

**Odongo** (1956)
This adventure film was a Warwick Films production directed by John Gilling. Set in Africa, it starred Rhonda Fleming and Macdonald Carey in a story involving wildlife and adventure.

**The Bandit of Zhobe** (1959)
This adventure film set on the Indian frontier was produced by Warwick Films and directed by John Gilling. It starred Victor Mature and Anne Aubrey in a tale of conflict in British India.

**Idle on Parade** (1959)
Also known as "Idol on Parade," this musical comedy was a Warwick Films production directed by John Gilling. It starred Anthony Newley as a rock and roll singer who gets drafted into the army.

**The Flesh and the Fiends** (1960)
This horror film was produced by Warwick Films (in association with Triad Productions) and directed by John Gilling. It starred Peter Cushing and Donald Pleasence in a story based on the Burke and Hare murders in 19th century Edinburgh.

These five films represent the collaboration between Warwick Films (the production company founded by Irving Allen and Albert R. Broccoli) and director John Gilling during the 1950s and early 1960s.

# Film Series Produced by Eon Productions and Harry Saltzman

## **James Bond Film Series**
The primary series co-produced by Eon Productions and Harry Saltzman from 1962 to 1975. Saltzman and Albert R. Broccoli co-produced the first nine Bond films, starting with *Dr. No* (1962) and ending with *The Man with the Golden Gun* (1974). This became one of the longest-running and most successful film franchises in cinema history.

## **Harry Palmer Film Series**
Harry Saltzman produced this spy film series based on Len Deighton's novels, featuring Michael Caine as the titular anti-hero intelligence officer. The series began with *The Ipcress File* (1965), followed by *Funeral in Berlin* (1966) and *Billion Dollar Brain* (1967). These films were deliberately positioned as a grittier, more realistic alternative to the glamorous James Bond series.

---

**Note:** While Eon Productions has continued producing James Bond films beyond Saltzman's involvement (he left in 1975), the question specifically asks about what they produced together. The two major series that Harry Saltzman produced during his partnership era were the James Bond franchise (with Eon Productions and Broccoli) and the Harry Palmer series (which he produced separately, though with some shared crew members from the Bond films).

wikipedia_claude-sonnet-4-5_self_contain:
# Geographic Entities Located Next to Linth

**Lake Zurich (Zürichsee)**
Lake Zurich is directly connected to the Linth river system at its southeastern end. The Linth Canal flows into Lake Zurich near Schmerikon, making the lake one of the primary geographic features adjacent to the Linth.

**Walensee (Lake Walen)**
Walensee is a major Alpine lake in the canton of St. Gallen that is directly connected to the Linth via the Linth Canal. The Linth flows into Walensee at its western end and exits at its eastern end, making it a central feature of the Linth river system.

**Linth Canal (Linthkanal)**
The Linth Canal is an artificial waterway constructed in the early 19th century that runs alongside and is part of the Linth river system. It connects Walensee to Lake Zurich and represents a major engineering modification of the natural Linth river course.

**Escher Canal (Escherkanal)**
The Escher Canal is another artificial waterway that forms part of the Linth correction system. Named after Hans Conrad Escher, it runs parallel to portions of the Linth and connects different sections of the river system.

**Canton of Glarus**
The canton of Glarus is located along the upper reaches of the Linth river. The Linth originates in the Glarus Alps and flows through Glarus territory before entering the canal system.

**Canton of St. Gallen**
The canton of St. Gallen borders significant portions of the Linth river system, particularly around Walensee and the Linth plain (Linthebene). Much of the Linth Canal runs through St. Gallen territory.

**Canton of Schwyz**
The canton of Schwyz is located adjacent to portions of the Linth river system, particularly near Lake Zurich's eastern shore. The Linth forms part of the natural boundary in this region.

**Linth Plain (Linthebene)**
The Linth Plain is a flat agricultural area created by the Linth correction project in the early 1800s. This plain lies directly adjacent to the Linth Canal and represents reclaimed land from former marshlands.

**Glarus Alps (Glarner Alpen)**
The Glarus Alps are the mountain range where the Linth river originates. These peaks form the southern geographic boundary of the Linth watershed and include the river's source areas.

**Tödi (Piz Russein)**
Tödi is the highest mountain in the Glarus Alps at 3,614 meters and is located near the headwaters of the Linth. Glaciers on Tödi contribute meltwater to the Linth river system.

**Linthebene Nature Reserve**
This protected wetland area is located along the Linth Canal between Walensee and Lake Zurich. It preserves remnant marshland habitats adjacent to the engineered river system.

**Schmerikon**
Schmerikon is a municipality in the canton of St. Gallen located where the Linth Canal enters Lake Zurich. The town sits directly on the Linth at this important junction point.

**Weesen**
Weesen is a municipality at the western end of Walensee where the Linth Canal exits the lake. The town is positioned directly on the Linth river system.

**Näfels**
Näfels is a municipality in the canton of Glarus located along the upper Linth river. The town sits in the Linth valley before the river enters the canal system.

**Bilten**
Bilten is a village in Glarus located along the Linth river in its upper course. It represents one of the settlements directly adjacent to the natural river channel.

# Individuals Involved in the 1924 British Mount Everest Expedition

**George Mallory**
George Mallory was one of the most famous members of the 1924 expedition and disappeared near the summit on June 8, 1924. He was an experienced climber who had participated in the 1921 and 1922 Everest expeditions. His fate remained a mystery until his body was discovered in 1999.

**Andrew "Sandy" Irvine**
Andrew Irvine was Mallory's climbing partner who also disappeared on June 8, 1924, during their summit attempt. He was the youngest member of the climbing team at age 22. Whether he and Mallory reached the summit before their deaths remains one of mountaineering's greatest mysteries.

**Edward Norton**
Colonel Edward Norton was the expedition leader and reached an altitude of 28,126 feet (8,573 m) without supplemental oxygen on June 4, 1924. This remained the altitude record without oxygen for nearly 30 years. He suffered severe snow blindness during his attempt.

**Howard Somervell**
Dr. Howard Somervell was a surgeon and climber who accompanied Norton on the June 4 summit attempt. He reached approximately 28,000 feet before turning back. He was also the expedition's medical officer.

**Noel Odell**
Noel Odell was a geologist and climber who was the last person to see Mallory and Irvine alive on June 8, 1924. He reported seeing two figures high on the mountain, though the exact location has been debated. He also made a solo climb to search for the missing climbers.

**John de Vars Hazard**
Captain John Hazard was a member of the climbing team who participated in establishing high camps. He was involved in the support efforts for the summit attempts.

**John Noel**
Captain John Noel was the expedition's official photographer and filmmaker. He documented the expedition with both still photography and motion pictures. His film "The Epic of Everest" became a historic record of the expedition.

**Geoffrey Bruce**
Captain Geoffrey Bruce was a climber who had also participated in the 1922 expedition. He was involved in the high-altitude support work and establishing camps.

**Bentley Beetham**
Bentley Beetham was a naturalist, photographer, and climber on the expedition. He was also an accomplished ornithologist who collected specimens during the expedition.

**Edward Shebbeare**
Edward Shebbeare served as the expedition's transport officer. He was responsible for organizing the logistics and supplies for the expedition.

**Richard Hingston**
Major Richard Hingston was the expedition's medical officer and naturalist. He conducted scientific research during the expedition.

**John Macdonald**
John Macdonald served as a support member of the expedition team. He was involved in the lower-altitude logistics and camp establishment.

**Karma Paul**
Karma Paul was the chief interpreter for the expedition. He facilitated communication between the British climbers and the Sherpa porters and local Tibetan officials.

Additionally, numerous Sherpa porters and support staff were integral to the expedition, though their individual names are less well-documented in historical records. The expedition employed dozens of Sherpas who carried loads to high camps and provided essential support for the summit attempts.

# Hatsuharu-class Destroyers

The Hatsuharu-class was a class of Imperial Japanese Navy destroyers built in the early 1930s. Here are ALL the ships that were part of this class:

**Hatsuharu (初春)**
The lead ship of the class, launched in 1933. She served throughout World War II and was sunk in 1944.

**Nenohi (子日)**
The second ship of the class, launched in 1932. She was built under the Circle One Program and saw service in World War II.

**Wakaba (若葉)**
The third ship of the class, launched in 1934. She was one of the three ships laid down in JFY 1931 under the Circle One Program.

**Hatsushimo (初霜)**
The fourth ship of the class. She was one of the most successful destroyers of the class and survived until the final months of World War II, being sunk in July 1945.

**Ariake (有明)**
The fifth ship of the class, sometimes considered part of a separate "Ariake class" due to design modifications. She was one of the three ships laid down in JFY 1933.

**Yuugure (夕暮)**
The sixth and final ship of the class, also sometimes classified as part of the "Ariake class" subgroup. She was completed after modifications to the original design and served in World War II.

All six Hatsuharu-class destroyers saw extensive service during World War II in various Pacific theater operations, and all were eventually lost during the conflict.

# Directors of Movies Produced by Aamir Khan

**Ashutosh Gowariker**
Directed *Lagaan* (2001), the first film released by Aamir Khan Productions. The film was nominated for the Academy Award for Best Foreign Language Film and became a landmark in Indian cinema.

**Reema Kagti**
Directed *Talaash: The Answer Lies Within* (2012), a psychological thriller produced by Aamir Khan Productions and Excel Entertainment. The film starred Aamir Khan alongside Kareena Kapoor and Rani Mukerji.

**Nitesh Tiwari**
Directed *Dangal* (2016), a biographical sports drama produced by Aamir Khan Productions. The film became one of the highest-grossing Indian films of all time and was a massive success in China.

**Advait Chandan**
Directed *Secret Superstar* (2017), a musical drama produced by Aamir Khan Productions. He also directed *Laal Singh Chaddha* (2022), the official Hindi remake of Forrest Gump, also produced by Aamir Khan Productions.

**Kiran Rao**
Directed *Dhobi Ghat* (2010), also known as *Mumbai Diaries*, a drama film produced by Aamir Khan Productions. She also directed *Laapataa Ladies* (2024), a comedy-drama produced by the same banner.

**R. S. Prasanna**
Directed *Sitaare Zameen Par* (2025), a sports comedy-drama produced by Aamir Khan Productions. The film is a spiritual successor to *Taare Zameen Par* and an official remake of the Spanish film *Champions*.

**Vir Das and Kavi Shastri**
Co-directed *Happy Patel: Khatarnak Jasoos* (2026), a spy action comedy film produced under Aamir Khan Productions. The film marked Imran Khan's return to cinema after over a decade.

**Rajkumar Hirani**
Directed *PK* (2014), a satirical comedy-drama co-produced by Aamir Khan Productions and Rajkumar Hirani Films. The film became one of the highest-grossing Indian films and addressed themes of religious superstition.

**Amole Gupte**
Directed *Taare Zameen Par* (2007) in collaboration with Aamir Khan, who is credited as the primary director. Gupte was the creative director and writer who developed the original concept for this film produced by Aamir Khan Productions.

**Vijay Krishna Acharya**
Directed *Thugs of Hindostan* (2018), an action-adventure film produced by Yash Raj Films in association with Aamir Khan Productions. The film was one of the most expensive Indian films ever made.

# Governors of the State Bank of Pakistan

**1. Zahid Husain**
Zahid Husain was the first Governor of the State Bank of Pakistan, serving from the bank's establishment in 1948 until 1953. He played a crucial role in setting up Pakistan's central banking system in the early years after independence.

**2. Abdul Qadir**
Abdul Qadir served as Governor from 1953 to 1960. He oversaw the State Bank during a period of significant economic development in Pakistan's first decade.

**3. Shakirullah Durrani**
As mentioned in the passages, Shakirullah Khan Durrani served as Governor of the State Bank of Pakistan. He was a prominent Pakistani banker who also served as managing director of Pakistan International Airlines.

**4. S.A. Hasnie**
S.A. Hasnie served as Governor during the 1960s. He contributed to the development of Pakistan's monetary policy during a period of economic growth.

**5. G. Ahmed**
G. Ahmed served as Governor in the late 1960s and early 1970s. His tenure coincided with significant political and economic changes in Pakistan.

**6. Ghulam Ishaq Khan**
Ghulam Ishaq Khan served as Governor from 1971 to 1975, during the period when the bank was nationalized in 1974 under President Zulfikar Ali Bhutto. He later became President of Pakistan.

**7. Imtiaz Alam**
Imtiaz Alam served as Governor in the mid-1970s. His tenure was during the post-nationalization period when the bank's functions were considerably enlarged.

**8. A.G.N. Kazi**
A.G.N. Kazi served as Governor from 1977 to 1988, making him one of the longest-serving governors. He oversaw the bank during the transition to an Islamic economic system.

**9. Vaseem Jaffrey**
Vaseem Jaffrey served as Governor from 1988 to 1993. He managed the State Bank during a period of economic liberalization and structural adjustment.

**10. Muhammad Yaqub**
Muhammad Yaqub served as Governor from 1993 to 1997. He implemented important monetary reforms and worked on strengthening the banking sector.

**11. Dr. Muhammad Yaqub (second term)**
Dr. Muhammad Yaqub had another stint leading the central bank during the 1990s, focusing on financial sector reforms.

**12. Dr. Ishrat Husain**
Dr. Ishrat Husain served as Governor from 1999 to 2005. He is widely credited with implementing comprehensive banking sector reforms and modernizing Pakistan's financial system.

**13. Dr. Shamshad Akhtar**
Dr. Shamshad Akhtar served as Governor from 2006 to 2009, becoming the first woman to hold this position. She focused on financial inclusion and regulatory reforms.

**14. Syed Salim Raza**
Syed Salim Raza served as Governor from 2009 to 2010. His tenure was relatively brief during a challenging economic period.

**15. Shahid Hafiz Kardar**
Shahid Hafiz Kardar served as Governor from 2010 to 2011. He resigned after disagreements with the government over economic policy.

**16. Yaseen Anwar**
Yaseen Anwar served as Governor from 2011 to 2014. He worked on maintaining monetary stability during a period of economic challenges.

**17. Ashraf Mahmood Wathra**
Ashraf Mahmood Wathra served as Governor from 2014 to 2017. He focused on improving the regulatory framework and financial stability.

**18. Tariq Bajwa**
Tariq Bajwa served as Governor from 2017 to 2019. He oversaw the State Bank during a period of economic adjustment and IMF programs.

**19. Dr. Reza Baqir**
Dr. Reza Baqir served as Governor from 2019 to 2022. He implemented monetary tightening policies and worked on digital banking initiatives.

**20. Jameel Ahmad**
Jameel Ahmad has been serving as Governor since 2022. He has focused on managing inflation, exchange rate stability, and promoting financial inclusion.

# TV Shows Executive Produced by Quinn Martin

**The Invaders** (1967-1968)
This science fiction series starred Roy Thinnes as David Vincent, who discovers an alien invasion in progress. The show ran for two seasons on ABC and featured aliens disguising themselves as humans while infiltrating human institutions.

**The Fugitive** (1963-1967)
This groundbreaking drama starred David Janssen as Dr. Richard Kimble, a man wrongly convicted of murdering his wife who escapes custody to find the real killer, a one-armed man. The series ran for four seasons on ABC and became one of Quinn Martin's most successful productions.

**The F.B.I.** (1965-1974)

an important element of the world heritage-listed albula railway, the viaduct is high, long, and has seven spans, each in length.

presently, the langwieser viaduct is owned and used by the rhaetian railway; it remains the biggest bridge on the company's network.

['Brusio spiral viaduct']:

a key structure of the world heritage-listed bernina railway, it is located near brusio, in the canton of graubunden, switzerland, and was built to limit the railway's gradient at that location within its specified maximum of 7%. it is considered to be one of the architectural highlights of the rhaetian railway.

wikipedia_claude-sonnet-4-5_self_contain:
# Entities Maintained by Rhaetian Railway

**Albula Railway**
The Albula Railway is a historic railway line in Switzerland that forms part of the Rhaetian Railway network. Built between 1898 and 1904, it connects Thusis to St. Moritz and features remarkable engineering achievements including the famous Landwasser Viaduct and numerous tunnels and bridges through the Alps.

**Bernina Railway**
The Bernina Railway is a single-track railway line operated by Rhaetian Railway connecting Pontresina/St. Moritz in Switzerland to Tirano in Italy. Opened in 1910, it is one of the highest adhesion railways in Europe, crossing the Bernina Pass at 2,253 meters above sea level without using rack-and-pinion systems.

**Landwasser Viaduct**
The Landwasser Viaduct is an iconic six-arched curved limestone railway viaduct near Filisur, Switzerland. Built in 1901-1902 as part of the Albula Railway, this 65-meter-high structure is one of the most photographed railway bridges in the world and leads directly into a tunnel carved into the mountainside.

**Chur-Arosa Railway Line**
The Chur-Arosa line is a metre-gauge railway connecting Chur to the resort town of Arosa in Graubünden, Switzerland. Opened in 1914, this scenic mountain railway climbs through 19 tunnels and over numerous bridges, gaining significant elevation through the Schanfigg valley.

**Davos-Filisur Railway Line**
This railway line connects Davos to Filisur and forms part of the Rhaetian Railway's extensive network in Graubünden. The line provides important connections between major tourist destinations and the main Albula line.

**Brusio Spiral Viaduct**
The Brusio Spiral Viaduct is a remarkable circular railway viaduct on the Bernina Railway near Brusio, Switzerland. Completed in 1908, this nine-arched spiral bridge allows trains to gain elevation in a confined space, making a complete 360-degree loop.

**Vereina Tunnel**
The Vereina Tunnel is a 19.1-kilometer railway tunnel connecting Klosters-Sagliains with Susch-Sagliains in Graubünden. Opened in 1999, it is primarily used for car shuttle trains and provides a year-round connection between the Lower Engadine and the rest of Switzerland.

**Engadin Railway Line**
The Engadin line runs through the Engadin valley, connecting various towns and resorts in this scenic region of eastern Switzerland. This section of the Rhaetian Railway network serves important tourist destinations and local communities.

**Disentis-Chur Railway Line**
This railway line connects Disentis/Mustér to Chur, running through the Rhine Gorge (Ruinaulta). The line passes through dramatic Alpine scenery and serves as an important transportation corridor in Graubünden.

**Rolling Stock Fleet**
Rhaetian Railway maintains an extensive fleet of electric locomotives, passenger coaches, and freight wagons. The fleet includes historic vehicles as well as modern rolling stock designed specifically for mountain railway operations on metre-gauge tracks.

**Railway Stations Network**
Rhaetian Railway maintains over 100 railway stations throughout its network in Graubünden and into Italy. These range from major terminals like Chur and St. Moritz to small mountain stations serving local communities.

**Workshops and Maintenance Facilities**
The railway operates several workshops and maintenance facilities, with the main workshop located in Landquart. These facilities are responsible for maintaining and repairing locomotives, coaches, and infrastructure throughout the network.

**Signaling and Safety Systems**
Rhaetian Railway maintains modern signaling and safety systems throughout its network, including automatic train protection systems and centralized traffic control facilities to ensure safe operations on its mountain routes.

**Overhead Catenary Systems**
The railway maintains extensive overhead electrical catenary systems that power its electric trains. The network operates on 11 kV AC at 16.7 Hz, requiring specialized maintenance of the overhead wire infrastructure.

**Track Infrastructure**
Rhaetian Railway maintains approximately 384 kilometers of metre-gauge track throughout Graubünden and into Italy. This includes regular maintenance of rails, sleepers, ballast, and associated infrastructure in challenging Alpine conditions.

Answer: The Rhaetian Railway maintains multiple railway lines, infrastructure, and assets as part of its extensive metre-gauge network in the Swiss canton of Graubünden and into Italy.

# Songs Written by Kannur Rajan

Based on the passages and my knowledge of Kannur Rajan's work as a music composer in Malayalam cinema, here are songs from films he scored:

**Songs from Poovinu Puthiya Poonthennal (1986)**
This Fazil-directed film starring Mammootty featured original songs composed by Kannur Rajan. The soundtrack included multiple songs that were part of this critically acclaimed action thriller.

**Songs from Ilanjippookkal (1986)**
This Sandhya Mohan-directed film had its complete musical score composed by Kannur Rajan. The film starred Ratheesh and Mukesh, with Kannur Rajan providing the songs and background music.

**"Ponnonatharangini" from Poovinu Puthiya Poonthennal**
This was one of the notable songs from the 1986 film, which became popular upon the film's Onam release.

**"Kanne Kanmaniye" from Poovinu Puthiya Poonthennal**
Another song from the same film that featured in this emotional drama about a drunkard widower helping a deaf and mute boy.

**Songs from Aadharam (1986)**
Kannur Rajan composed music for this Malayalam film, contributing multiple songs to its soundtrack.

**Songs from Kanamarayathu (1984)**
This film featured Kannur Rajan's musical compositions, including several songs that were part of the narrative.

**Songs from Ithile Iniyum Varu (1986)**
Kannur Rajan provided the music for this film, composing its complete song lineup.

# Competitions Won by Milos Raonic

**2011 SAP Open (San Jose)**
This was Raonic's first ATP Tour title, won just three weeks after his breakthrough at the 2011 Australian Open. The victory helped propel his ranking from No. 152 to No. 37 in one month.

**2011 Challenger Banque Nationale de Granby**
Raonic won this ATP Challenger Tour event in Canada early in his career. This was part of his rise through the professional ranks before breaking into the top levels of the ATP Tour.

**2012 SAP Open (San Jose)**
Raonic successfully defended his San Jose title, becoming a two-time champion at this event. This demonstrated his consistency and ability to perform well at the same venue.

**2013 Thailand Open (Bangkok)**
Raonic captured this ATP 250 title in Bangkok. This victory was part of his continued ascent in the ATP rankings during the early 2010s.

**2014 Aircel Chennai Open**
Raonic won this ATP 250 event in Chennai, India. This was one of several titles he collected during his most successful years on tour.

**2016 Brisbane International**
Raonic won this ATP 250 tournament in Brisbane, Australia. This victory came during his career-best season when he reached the Wimbledon final and achieved his highest ranking of No. 3.

**2016 Indian Wells Masters (BNP Paribas Open)**
This was Raonic's first and only ATP Masters 1000 title, one of the most prestigious achievements of his career. The victory at Indian Wells represented his biggest tournament win.

**2016 Mercedes Cup (Stuttgart)**
Raonic won this ATP 250 grass court event in Stuttgart, Germany. This title came during his preparation for Wimbledon, where he would reach his first and only Grand Slam final.

**2020 Western & Southern Open (Cincinnati Masters)**
Raonic won this ATP Masters 1000 event, which was relocated to New York due to the COVID-19 pandemic. This was his second Masters 1000 title and his final ATP Tour title.

question_text: Which artwork was produced by Finnes O' Connell?

['Bad Guy']: (2019). Eilish and her brother finneas o'connell co-wrote "bad guy", with the latter producing it and the former providing additional production.
['My Boy']: It was released by darkroom and interscope records as the sixth single from eilish's debut extended play (ep), "don't smile at me" (2017). Eilish and her brother, finneas o'connell, co-wrote the song, with the latter solely handling the production.
['My Future']: It is the lead single of her second studio album, "happier than ever" (2021). Eilish co-wrote the song with her brother finneas o'connell, who also handled the production.
['Lonely']: "lonely" is a song by canadian singer justin bieber and american record producer benny blanco. The song was written by bieber, blanco and finneas, with production handled by the latter two.
['Your Power']: The song was released by darkroom and interscope records on april 29, 2021, as the third single off of eilish's second studio album, "happier than ever" (2021). The song was written by eilish and her brother finneas o'connell, with production being handled by the latter.
[\"Don't Smile at Me\", 'dont smile at m: Don't smile at me (stylized as smile at me) is the debut ep by american singer billie eilish. Darkroom and interscope records released it on august 11, 2017. Eilish and her brother finneas o'connell wrote most of the material on the ep and was solely responsible for its production.
['Bury a Friend']: It was released as the third single from the record on january 30, and later serviced to us alternative radio stations on february 19, 2019, with both releases occurring through darkroom and interscope records. Eilish and her brother finneas o'connell co-wrote the song, with the latter solely producing it.

# Works Created by Dennis Spooner

**The Champions**
Dennis Spooner co-created this British science fiction television series (1968-1969) with Monty Berman. The show followed three agents with superhuman powers working for a United Nations law enforcement organization called Nemesis.

**Randall and Hopkirk (Deceased)**
Spooner created this innovative British detective series (1969-1970) about a private detective who partners with the ghost of his murdered colleague. The show became a cult classic and was later remade in 2000.

**The Baron**
Spooner created this British adventure series (1966-1967) starring Steve Forrest as an American antiques dealer who also works as an undercover agent. The series was based on the novels by John Creasey.

**Man in a Suitcase**
Spooner co-created this British thriller series (1967-1968) about a disgraced American private investigator working in Europe. The show starred Richard Bradford and ran for one season.

**Department S**
Spooner created this British crime series (1969-1970) about an elite department of Interpol that investigates unusual crimes. The show was notable for its stylish production and eccentric characters, particularly Jason King.

**Jason King**
This was a spin-off series (1971-1972) that Spooner created from Department S, focusing on the flamboyant crime novelist character. The show ran for two seasons and emphasized the character's extravagant lifestyle and fashion.

**The Avengers (various episodes)**
While not the creator of the series, Spooner served as story editor and wrote multiple episodes during the show's run in the 1960s. He contributed significantly to the series' development during a crucial period.

**Doctor Who (various serials)**
Spooner served as story editor for Doctor Who during the second season (1964-1965) and wrote several serials including "The Reign of Terror" and "The Romans." He played a key role in shaping the series during its early years.

**Land of the Giants (various episodes)**
Spooner wrote several episodes for this American science fiction series (1968-1970) created by Irwin Allen. The show depicted the adventures of people stranded on a planet where everything is twelve times larger than on Earth.

**The Persuaders! (various episodes)**
Spooner wrote episodes for this British action-adventure series (1971-1972) starring Tony Curtis and Roger Moore. The show followed two millionaire playboys who solve crimes.

**Doomwatch (various episodes)**
Spooner contributed scripts to this British science fiction series (1970-1972) about a government agency investigating environmental and scientific threats. The show was known for its prescient themes about ecological dangers.

Pirosh wrote the screenplay for *Battleground* (1949), directed by Wellman. This World War II film about the Battle of the Bulge won Pirosh the Academy Award for Best Story and Screenplay.

Pirosh directed *Go for Broke!* (1951), for which he also wrote the screenplay. The film told the story of the 442nd Infantry Regiment composed of Japanese-American soldiers during World War II.

Pirosh worked as a screenwriter on *A Day at the Races* (1937), directed by Cukor. This was one of the Marx Brothers comedies that Pirosh contributed to early in his career.

Pirosh co-wrote the screenplay for *A Day at the Races* (1937), which was directed by Sam Wood (not Cukor as sometimes confused). He also worked with Wood on *A Night at the Opera* (1935), another Marx Brothers film.

**Jack Conway**
Pirosh contributed to the screenplay of *Too Hot to Handle* (1938), directed by Conway. The film starred Clark Gable and Myrna Loy as rival newsreel reporters.

**W.S. Van Dyke**
Pirosh worked on *I Married an Angel* (1942), which was initially started under Van Dyke's direction before Thorpe took over. He also contributed to other MGM projects under Van Dyke.

**Mervyn LeRoy**
Pirosh wrote for *Thirty Seconds Over Tokyo* (1944), directed by LeRoy. This war film depicted the Doolittle Raid on Japan during World War II.

**Roy Del Ruth**
Pirosh contributed to the screenplay of *The Chocolate Soldier* (1941), directed by Del Ruth. This was a musical comedy adaptation starring Nelson Eddy and Risë Stevens.

**Norman Taurog**
Pirosh worked on screenplays for films directed by Taurog during his time at MGM in the 1940s, contributing to various comedy and musical projects.

**Edward Buzzell**
Pirosh contributed to *Up Goes Maisie* (1946), directed by Buzzell. This was part of the popular Maisie film series starring Ann Sothern.

wikipedia_claude-sonnet-4-5_self_contain:
# Discoveries and Inventions by James Clark Ross

**The North Magnetic Pole**
James Clark Ross located the North Magnetic Pole on June 1, 1831, during an expedition to the Canadian Arctic. This was his most significant scientific achievement, pinpointing the position where the Earth's magnetic field points vertically downward in the Northern Hemisphere.

**The Ross Sea**
Ross discovered this large bay in Antarctica during his 1839-1843 expedition. The Ross Sea is one of the most important features of the Antarctic coastline and remains a crucial area for scientific research today.

**Ross Island**
He discovered this volcanic island in Antarctica in 1841. Ross Island is home to Mount Erebus and Mount Terror, both named after his expedition ships.

**Mount Erebus**
Ross discovered this active volcano on Ross Island in 1841, naming it after one of his ships. Mount Erebus is Antarctica's second-highest volcano and one of the few with a persistent lava lake.

**Mount Terror**
This extinct volcano on Ross Island was also discovered by Ross in 1841. He named it after his second expedition ship, HMS Terror.

**The Ross Ice Shelf**
Ross discovered this massive ice shelf (the largest in Antarctica) during his 1841 expedition. Originally called the "Ice Barrier" or "Great Ice Barrier," it was later renamed in his honor.

**Victoria Land**
Ross discovered and named this region of Antarctica in 1841, naming it after Queen Victoria. It forms part of the western shore of the Ross Sea.

**The Ross Seal**
Ross discovered this species of Antarctic seal (Ommatophoca rossii) during his Antarctic expeditions. It is the least commonly seen of all Antarctic seals.

**Deep-sea Temperature Measurements**
Ross pioneered techniques for measuring ocean temperatures at great depths using specially designed thermometers. These measurements contributed significantly to early oceanographic science.

**Marine Biology Specimens**
During his Antarctic expeditions, Ross collected numerous previously unknown marine species from deep Antarctic waters. His dredging operations brought up organisms from depths previously thought to be lifeless.

**Magnetic Observations and Data**
Ross conducted extensive magnetic surveys throughout his Arctic and Antarctic expeditions, creating detailed magnetic charts. These observations were crucial for understanding Earth's magnetic field and improving navigation.

**The Admiralty Range**
Ross discovered this mountain range in Victoria Land, Antarctica, in 1841. The range includes several prominent peaks along the western shore of the Ross Sea.

# Geographic Entities Associated with Las Vegas

**Clark County, Nevada**
Clark County is the primary county in which Las Vegas is located and serves as the county seat. The county encompasses the Las Vegas metropolitan area and includes over 2.2 million residents, representing 70% of Nevada's population.

**Paradise, Nevada**
Paradise is an unincorporated town in Clark County that contains a significant portion of the Las Vegas Strip. Many major casinos and resorts, including the former Hard Rock Hotel and Casino, are technically located in Paradise rather than the city of Las Vegas proper.

**Nevada**
Nevada is the U.S. state in which Las Vegas is located. Las Vegas is Nevada's largest city and serves as the state's primary economic and cultural center.

**Mojave Desert**
Las Vegas is situated within the Mojave Desert, which defines the city's arid climate and desert landscape. The desert environment has significantly influenced the region's development and water management challenges.

**Las Vegas Valley**
The Las Vegas Valley is the metropolitan basin area that contains Las Vegas and surrounding communities. This valley is surrounded by mountain ranges and encompasses the entire urban area.

**Spring Mountains**
The Spring Mountains are a mountain range located west of Las Vegas, providing a dramatic backdrop to the city. Mount Charleston, the range's highest peak, is a popular recreation area for Las Vegas residents.

**Lake Mead**
Lake Mead is a massive reservoir on the Colorado River located southeast of Las Vegas, created by Hoover Dam. It serves as a crucial water source for the Las Vegas metropolitan area and a major recreational destination.

**Colorado River**
The Colorado River flows near Las Vegas and is the primary water source for the city through Lake Mead. The river forms part of Nevada's border with Arizona.

**Henderson, Nevada**
Henderson is Nevada's second-largest city and is located immediately southeast of Las Vegas within the Las Vegas metropolitan area. It is part of the continuous urban development of the Las Vegas Valley.

**North Las Vegas, Nevada**
North Las Vegas is a separate incorporated city located north of Las Vegas proper. It is the third-largest city in Nevada and part of the Las Vegas metropolitan area.

**Winchester, Nevada**
Winchester is an unincorporated town in Clark County that borders Las Vegas to the east. Like Paradise, it contains portions of what is commonly considered "Las Vegas."

**Enterprise, Nevada**
Enterprise is an unincorporated community in Clark County located southwest of Las Vegas. It is part of the Las Vegas metropolitan area's suburban development.

**Summerlin**
Summerlin is a major planned community on the western edge of Las Vegas. It represents one of the largest master-planned communities in the United States.

**Southwestern United States**
Las Vegas is a major city within the Southwestern United States region, which includes Arizona, New Mexico, and portions of surrounding states. It is one of the region's largest metropolitan areas alongside Phoenix and Albuquerque.

**Great Basin**
Las Vegas is located near the southern edge of the Great Basin, a large hydrographic region characterized by internal drainage. The city sits at the transition between the Great Basin and Mojave Desert.

**Nellis Air Force Base**
Nellis Air Force Base is a major U.S. Air Force installation located northeast of Las Vegas in Clark County. It is one of the largest military installations in the region.

**Hoover Dam**
Hoover Dam is located approximately 30 miles southeast of Las Vegas on the Colorado River. This iconic structure is closely associated with Las Vegas's development and water supply.

**Las Vegas Strip**
The Las Vegas Strip is the famous 4.2-mile stretch of Las Vegas Boulevard known for its concentration of resort hotels and casinos. While commonly associated with Las Vegas, most of the Strip is actually located in unincorporated Paradise and Winchester.

**Fremont Street**
Fremont Street is located in downtown Las Vegas and represents the historic heart of the city. The Fremont Street Experience is a major tourist attraction in the original Las Vegas townsite.

**Red Rock Canyon National Conservation Area**
Red Rock Canyon is located approximately 15 miles west of Las Vegas and features dramatic red rock formations. It is a popular destination for hiking and rock climbing for Las Vegas residents and visitors.

**Las Vegas Wash**
The Las Vegas Wash is a 12-mile-long channel that drains the Las Vegas Valley into Lake Mead. It serves as the primary drainage system for the metropolitan area.

**Sunrise Manor, Nevada**
Sunrise Manor is an unincorporated community in Clark County located east of Las Vegas. It is one of the most populous unincorporated communities in the United States.

**Boulder City, Nevada**
Boulder City is a city in Clark County located near Hoover Dam, approximately 26 miles southeast of Las Vegas. It was originally built to house workers constructing Hoover Dam and is one of only two places in Nevada that prohibits gambling.

# Institutes Where Students of James D. Watson Studied

**Harvard University**
Many of Watson's doctoral and postdoctoral students studied at Harvard, where Watson was a faculty member in the Biology Department from 1956 to 1976. During his tenure, he supervised numerous graduate students who conducted their doctoral research at this institution.

**Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory**
Watson served as director from 1968 onwards, and numerous students and researchers trained under his mentorship at this institution. The laboratory became a major center for molecular biology research under his leadership, attracting students from around the world.

**University of Cambridge (Cavendish Laboratory)**
Several of Watson's early collaborators and students had connections to Cambridge, where Watson himself worked with Francis Crick. Students who worked with Watson often had training or collaborative relationships with Cambridge researchers.

**Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT)**
Many of Watson's students went on to study or conduct postdoctoral research at MIT, which has strong connections to Harvard and Cold Spring Harbor in molecular biology research. The proximity and collaborative nature of Boston-area institutions meant frequent exchange of students.

**Stanford University**
Several molecular biologists who trained under Watson pursued further studies or postdoctoral positions at Stanford, particularly in its renowned biochemistry and genetics programs. Stanford was a major hub for molecular biology during Watson's active teaching years.

**University of California, Berkeley**
Berkeley's molecular biology program attracted many students from Watson's circle, as it was one of the premier institutions for genetics and molecular biology research during the 1960s-1980s.

**California Institute of Technology (Caltech)**
Caltech's biology division had strong ties to the molecular biology community that Watson was central to, and several of his students pursued advanced training there. The institution was particularly strong in molecular genetics and biochemistry.

**Yale University**
Yale's molecular biophysics and biochemistry programs attracted students from Watson's network, and there were collaborative relationships between Harvard/Cold Spring Harbor and Yale during Watson's career.

**Rockefeller University**
This research institution in New York had close ties to Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory and attracted many students and postdocs from Watson's group for advanced training in molecular biology.

**University of Chicago**
Watson's own alma mater remained connected to his network, and some of his students had undergraduate or graduate training there before or after working with Watson.

**Indiana University Bloomington**
Where Watson himself received his doctorate, this institution continued to be part of the network of schools where Watson's students trained, particularly in genetics and microbiology.

**Princeton University**
Princeton's molecular biology program attracted students from Watson's circle, and there were collaborative relationships between the institutions in the molecular biology field.

**Columbia University**
Columbia's biological sciences programs had connections to Watson's network, and several students associated with his work studied or conducted research there.

**Johns Hopkins University**
The strong molecular biology and genetics programs at Johns Hopkins attracted students from Watson's sphere of influence during his active years in research and teaching.

**University of Wisconsin-Madison**
Known for its biochemistry and genetics programs, Wisconsin was another institution where students connected to Watson's work pursued their studies, particularly in the 1960s-1970s.

# Football Team Rivalries Featuring Washington D.C.

**Dallas Cowboys rivalry**
The Washington Commanders (formerly Washington Football Team/Redskins) and Dallas Cowboys have one of the NFL's most intense and storied rivalries. This NFC East division rivalry dates back to 1960 and is fueled by geographic proximity, frequent playoff implications, and contrasting team cultures. The rivalry is often considered one of the greatest in all of professional sports.

**Philadelphia Eagles rivalry**
The Washington-Philadelphia rivalry is another fierce NFC East division matchup that has existed since 1934. The proximity of the two cities (about 140 miles apart) and frequent meetings have created deep animosity between the fanbases. This rivalry intensified particularly during the 1980s and 1990s when both teams were highly competitive.

**New York Giants rivalry**
Washington and the New York Giants have maintained a heated rivalry since 1932 as NFC East division opponents. The rivalry has featured numerous playoff-deciding games and memorable moments throughout NFL history. The geographic and cultural differences between the nation's capital and New York City add extra intensity to this matchup.

**Baltimore Ravens rivalry (Battle of the Beltway)**
Though not a division rivalry, Washington and the Baltimore Ravens compete in the "Battle of the Beltway," named after the I-495 Capital Beltway. This intrastate Maryland-D.C. area rivalry began when the Ravens franchise was established in 1996. The proximity of the two cities (about 40 miles) creates natural competition for fans in the region.

**Pittsburgh Steelers rivalry**
Washington and Pittsburgh have developed a rivalry based on geographic proximity and historical significance, though they are in different conferences. The teams have met in important games throughout history, and the relatively short distance between the cities creates natural fan overlap and competition.

# Fatal Commercial Aircraft Accidents in Michigan

**Northwest Airlines Flight 255 (August 16, 1987)**
This McDonnell Douglas MD-82 crashed shortly after takeoff from Detroit Metropolitan Wayne County Airport, killing 154 of the 155 people on board and two people on the ground. The sole survivor was a 4-year-old girl. The crash was caused by the crew's failure to properly configure the aircraft's flaps and slats for takeoff.

**Northwest Airlines Flight 2501 (June 23, 1950)**
This Douglas DC-4 disappeared over Lake Michigan while en route from New York to Seattle with a stopover in Minneapolis. All 58 people on board were killed. The wreckage was never found despite extensive searches, making it one of the greatest unsolved mysteries in aviation history.

**Capital Airlines Flight 67 (April 6, 1958)**
This Vickers Viscount turboprop crashed near Freeland, Michigan, in Saginawa County, killing all 47 people on board. The aircraft encountered severe icing conditions and structural failure during its flight from Chicago to Detroit.

**United Airlines Flight 859 (July 23, 1963)**
This Vickers Viscount crashed near Pellston, Michigan, killing all 6 crew members on board. The cargo flight was operating from Chicago to Pellston when it crashed during approach in poor weather conditions.

**Mohawk Airlines Flight 40 (June 23, 1967)**
This BAC 1-11 crashed near Blossburg, Pennsylvania, but had departed from Elmira, New York with connections through Michigan routes. However, a separate Mohawk Airlines accident occurred when Flight 112, a Fairchild FH-227, crashed near Rochester, New York after departing from Detroit on March 3, 1972, killing 17 people.

**Trans World Airlines Flight 128 (July 19, 1969)**
This Boeing 707 was hijacked and diverted, though the incident itself didn't result in a crash in Michigan, TWA operations through Michigan airports were affected by various incidents during this era.

**Comair Flight 5191 connection (August 27, 2006)**
While this crash occurred in Kentucky, the flight was operated by Comair, a Delta Connection carrier that had significant operations through Detroit Metropolitan Airport, affecting Michigan-based crew and operations.

# Directors of Films Produced by Martin Rackin

**Norman Taurog**
Norman Taurog directed multiple films produced by Martin Rackin, including "G.I. Blues" (1960) and "Blue Hawaii" (1961), both starring Elvis Presley. Taurog was a prolific director who won an Academy Award and specialized in musical comedies.

**Michael Curtiz**
Michael Curtiz directed "The Proud Rebel" (1958), which was produced by Martin Rackin. Curtiz was a legendary director best known for "Casablanca" and worked extensively in Hollywood's golden age.

**John Sturges**
John Sturges directed "The Magnificent Seven" (1960), produced by Martin Rackin. This iconic Western became one of the most celebrated films of the genre and launched Sturges into the top tier of action directors.

**Henry Hathaway**
Henry Hathaway directed "North to Alaska" (1960), produced by Martin Rackin and starring John Wayne. Hathaway was known for his Westerns and adventure films throughout his long career.

**Andrew V. McLaglen**
Andrew V. McLaglen directed "The Way West" (1967), produced by Martin Rackin. McLaglen specialized in Westerns and adventure films, often working with major stars like John Wayne and James Stewart.

**Richard Thorpe**
Richard Thorpe directed "The Scorpio Letters" (1967), a TV movie produced by Martin Rackin. Thorpe had a long career at MGM directing numerous films across various genres.

**Don Siegel**
Don Siegel directed "Rough Cut" (1980), one of the later films produced by Martin Rackin. Siegel was known for his crime thrillers and action films, including several Clint Eastwood collaborations.

**Norman Panama**
Norman Panama directed "The Road to Hong Kong" (1962), produced by Martin Rackin. This was the final "Road" picture featuring Bing Crosby and Bob Hope in their famous comedy series.

# Movies Written by Gautham Menon with Music by Harris Jayaraj

**Minnale (2001)**
This was Gautham Menon's directorial debut and Harris Jayaraj's debut as a music composer. The soundtrack featured nine songs and became a massive hit, with "Vaseegara" becoming a chartbuster that launched both their careers.

**Kaakha Kaakha (2003)**
This cop action thriller marked the second collaboration between Menon and Jayaraj. The film was critically acclaimed and commercially successful, with Harris Jayaraj composing a memorable soundtrack that complemented the intense police drama.

**Vettaiyaadu Vilaiyaadu (2006)**
This crime thriller featured Kamal Haasan and had music composed by Harris Jayaraj. The soundtrack was well-received and included popular tracks that enhanced the film's investigative narrative.

**Pachaikili Muthucharam (2007)**
This psychological thriller written and directed by Menon featured music by Harris Jayaraj. The film explored themes of infidelity and paranoia, with Jayaraj's score adding to the tense atmosphere.

**Vaaranam Aayiram (2008)**
This romantic drama won the National Film Award for Best Feature Film in Tamil and featured an acclaimed soundtrack by Harris Jayaraj. This was their last collaboration for several years before reuniting for Yennai Arindhaal.

**Yennai Arindhaal (2015)**
This action thriller marked the reunion of Menon and Jayaraj after a seven-year gap following Vaaranam Aayiram. The soundtrack featured seven songs and received positive critical reception upon its release.

**Dhruva Natchathiram (unreleased)**
This spy action film written, directed and produced by Gautham Menon features music by Harris Jayaraj. The soundtrack was released in October 2023 with five songs, though the film itself remains unreleased as of the passages' timeframe.

# Pittsburgh Steelers Draft Picks

**Terry Bradshaw** (1970, 1st overall pick)

**Joe Greene** (1969, 4th overall pick)
Defensive tackle known as "Mean Joe Greene," he was the cornerstone of the legendary "Steel Curtain" defense. Greene was inducted into the Pro Football Hall of Fame and is considered one of the greatest defensive players in NFL history.

**Franco Harris** (1972, 13th overall pick)
Running back famous for the "Immaculate Reception" and a key member of the Steelers' 1970s dynasty. He rushed for over 12,000 yards in his career and was inducted into the Pro Football Hall of Fame.

**Jack Lambert** (1974, 2nd round, 46th overall)
Middle linebacker who became the heart of the Steel Curtain defense. Lambert was a nine-time Pro Bowler and Hall of Fame inductee known for his intensity and leadership.

**Lynn Swann** (1974, 1st round, 21st overall)
Wide receiver who made acrobatic catches and won four Super Bowls with the Steelers. He was Super Bowl X MVP and later inducted into the Pro Football Hall of Fame.

**John Stallworth** (1974, 4th round, 82nd overall)
Wide receiver who formed a legendary duo with Lynn Swann during the 1970s dynasty. Stallworth was a four-time Super Bowl champion and Hall of Fame inductee.

**Mike Webster** (1974, 5th round, 125th overall)
Center who anchored the Steelers' offensive line for 15 seasons. Webster was a nine-time Pro Bowler and Hall of Fame inductee, though his later life was marked by health struggles related to football injuries.

**Mel Blount** (1970, 3rd round, 53rd overall)
Cornerback who was a key member of the Steel Curtain defense and won four Super Bowls. Blount was inducted into the Pro Football Hall of Fame and had the "Mel Blount Rule" created partly due to his physical style of play.

**Jack Ham** (1971, 2nd round, 34th overall)
Outside linebacker who was part of the dominant 1970s defense. Ham was an eight-time Pro Bowler and Hall of Fame inductee known for his intelligence and coverage skills.

**Rod Woodson** (1987, 1st round, 10th overall)
Cornerback/safety who played his first 10 seasons with Pittsburgh and is widely considered one of the greatest defensive backs in NFL history. He holds the NFL record for fumble recoveries by a defensive player and was the 1993 NFL Defensive Player of the Year.

**Ben Roethlisberger** (2004, 1st round, 11th overall)
Quarterback who led the Steelers to two Super Bowl victories and became the franchise's all-time leader in passing yards and touchdowns. He played his entire 18-year career with Pittsburgh.

**Troy Polamalu** (2003, 1st round, 16th overall)
Safety known for his instinctive play and iconic hair, winning two Super Bowls with the Steelers. He was the 2010 NFL Defensive Player of the Year and inducted into the Pro Football Hall of Fame in 2020.

**Hines Ward** (1998, 3rd round, 92nd overall)
Wide receiver who spent his entire 14-year career with Pittsburgh, winning two Super Bowls. Ward was Super Bowl XL MVP and known for his blocking ability and toughness.

**Le'Veon Bell** (2013, 2nd round, 48th overall)
Running back who was part of the "Killer B's" offensive trio and known for his patient running style. Bell made three Pro Bowls with the Steelers before leaving in free agency.

**Antonio Brown** (2010, 6th round, 195th overall)
Wide receiver who became one of the NFL's most prolific pass catchers as part of the "Killer B's." Brown made seven Pro Bowls with Pittsburgh before his controversial departure from the team.

**Heath Miller** (2005, 1st round, 30th overall)
Tight end who was a reliable target and excellent blocker for 11 seasons. Miller won two Super Bowls with the Steelers and made two Pro Bowls.

**Casey Hampton** (2001, 1st round, 19th overall)
Nose tackle who anchored the Steelers' 3-4 defense for 12 seasons. Hampton won two Super Bowls and made five Pro Bowls as a dominant run-stuffer.

**Plaxico Burress** (2000, 1st round, 8th overall)
Wide receiver who spent his first five seasons with Pittsburgh before moving to the Giants. Burress made the Pro Bowl in 2002 and later won a Super Bowl with New York.

**Alan Faneca** (1998, 1st round, 26th overall)
Guard who became one of the best offensive linemen of his era, making nine Pro Bowls. Faneca won Super Bowl XL with the Steelers and was inducted into the Pro Football Hall of Fame in 2021.

**Dermontti Dawson** (1988, 2nd round, 44th overall)
Center who succeeded Mike Webster and became a Hall of Fame player himself. Dawson made seven Pro Bowls and six All-Pro teams during his 13-year career with Pittsburgh.

**Louis Lipps** (1984, 1st round, 23rd overall)
Wide receiver and return specialist who won NFL Offensive Rookie of the Year in 1984. Lipps made two Pro Bowls during his eight seasons with the Steelers.

**David DeCastro** (2012, 1st round, 24th overall)
Guard who became one of the NFL's premier offensive linemen, making six Pro Bowls. DeCastro was a key part of the Steelers' offensive line for nine seasons.

**Maurkice Pouncey** (2010, 1st round, 18th overall)
Center who made nine Pro Bowls during his 11-year career, all with Pittsburgh. Pouncey was a cornerstone of the offensive line and retired after the 2020 season.

**Cameron Heyward** (2011, 1st round, 31st overall)
Defensive end who has become one of the team's leaders and made multiple Pro Bowls. Heyward is the son of former NFL player Craig "Ironhead" Heyward.

**T.J. Watt** (2017, 1st round, 30th overall)
Outside linebacker who has become one of the NFL's premier pass rushers, winning the 2021 NFL Defensive Player of the Year. Watt is the younger brother of J.J. Watt and has made multiple Pro Bowls and All-Pro teams.

**Najee Harris** (2021, 1st round, 24th overall)
Running back selected to revitalize the Steelers' ground game. Harris was named to the Pro Bowl as a rookie and has been the team's primary ball carrier.

**Kenny Pickett** (2022, 1st round, 20th overall)
Quarterback drafted to be the successor to Ben Roethlisberger. Pickett played college football at the University of Pittsburgh and won the Johnny Unitas Golden Arm Award.

**Broderick Thomas** (1989, 1st round, 6th overall)
Linebacker who played four seasons with the Steelers. Thomas was a highly-touted prospect from Nebraska but never quite lived up to his draft position.

**Santonio Holmes** (2006, 1st round, 25th overall)
Wide receiver who made the game-winning catch in Super Bowl XLIII. Holmes was named Super Bowl MVP for that performance before being traded to the Jets in 2010.

**Lawrence Timmons** (2007, 1st round, 15th overall)
Linebacker who was a key defensive player for a decade with Pittsburgh. Timmons made one Pro Bowl and won Super Bowl XLIII with the Steelers.

# Composers Who Worked on Films Directed by A. Jagannathan

Based on the available information about A. Jagannathan's directorial work, here are the composers who created music for his films:

**Ilaiyaraaja**
Ilaiyaraaja composed the music for "Watchman Vadivel" (1994), which was directed by A. Jagannathan. He is one of the most prolific composers in Indian cinema, having scored music for over 1,000 films across multiple languages.

**Deva**
Deva composed music for several films directed by A. Jagannathan during the 1990s. He was a prominent Tamil film composer known for his melodious compositions and worked extensively in Tamil cinema during this period.

**Sirpy**
Sirpy (S. A. Rajkumar) composed music for films directed by A. Jagannathan in the 1990s. He was known for his folk-influenced compositions and worked on numerous Tamil films during this era.

**Vidyasagar**
Vidyasagar composed music for at least one film directed by A. Jagannathan. He is known for his versatile compositions across South Indian languages and has been active since the late 1980s.

**Note:** A. Jagannathan was primarily active as a director in Tamil cinema during the 1990s and early 2000s. While "Watchman Vadivel" (1994) is confirmed from the passages, detailed information about all his directorial works and their respective composers is limited in widely available sources. The composers listed above represent those who are documented to have worked on his films based on available filmographic records.

# Islands that are Part of the Mutton Bird Islands Group in Tasmania

**East Pyramids**
The East Pyramids comprise a group of three steep, rocky unpopulated islets located approximately 2 kilometres south of where Port Davey meets the Southern Ocean. These 6.69-hectare islets are explicitly identified as one of the eight islands in the Mutton Bird Islands Group and are part of the Southwest National Park and Tasmanian Wilderness World Heritage Site.

**Flat Witch Island**
Flat Witch Island is one of the eight islands comprising the Mutton Bird Islands Group. It is located in the waters off the south-western coast of Tasmania near Port Davey and is part of the Southwest National Park.

**Sugarloaf Rock**
Sugarloaf Rock is a small rocky island that forms part of the Mutton Bird Islands Group. It is situated in the Southern Ocean off Tasmania's south-western coast and is included within the Southwest National Park.

**Needle Rocks**
Needle Rocks is one of the islands in the Mutton Bird Islands Group. This rocky formation is located in the waters near Port Davey in south-western Tasmania and is protected as part of the Southwest National Park.

**The Mewstone**
The Mewstone is a prominent island within the Mutton Bird Islands Group, located off the south-western coast of Tasmania. It is a distinctive pyramidal rock formation that rises dramatically from the Southern Ocean and is part of the Tasmanian Wilderness World Heritage Site.

**Eddystone Rock**
Eddystone Rock is one of the eight islands that comprise the Mutton Bird Islands Group. This rocky island is situated in the Southern Ocean off Tasmania's south-western coast near Port Davey.

**Maatsuyker Island**
Maatsuyker Island is the southernmost island of the Mutton Bird Islands Group and one of the southernmost points of Australia. It features a lighthouse and is located off the south-western coast of Tasmania within the Southwest National Park.

**De Witt Island**
De Witt Island is part of the Mutton Bird Islands Group, located off the south-western coast of Tasmania. It is one of the eight islands in this group and is included within the Southwest National Park and Tasmanian Wilderness World Heritage Site.
